title: Kinematics
objectives: Define displacement, velocity, and acceleration as vector quantities, distinguishing them from their scalar counterparts.
Apply the equations of uniform acceleration (v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as) to solve problems in one dimension.
Describe projectile motion as the combination of independent horizontal uniform motion and vertical uniformly accelerated motion.
Explain the effect of fluid resistance on the motion of a falling object, including the concept of terminal velocity.
Determine the gradient of a displacement–time graph as velocity and the gradient of a velocity–time graph as acceleration.
Apply graphical and algebraic methods to analyse motion in two dimensions, including relative velocity (HL).
summary:
Kinematics is the study of motion without reference to its causes. The key quantities are displacement (s), a vector representing change in position; velocity (v), the rate of change of displacement; and acceleration (a), the rate of change of velocity. Speed and distance are the corresponding scalar magnitudes. Average velocity is defined as v_avg = Δs/Δt, while instantaneous velocity is the gradient of the displacement–time graph at a point.

For uniformly accelerated motion (constant a), the four kinematic equations connect displacement, initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration, and time: v = u + at; s = ut + ½at²; v² = u² + 2as; s = ½(u + v)t. These are derived from the definitions of acceleration and average velocity. On a velocity–time graph, displacement is the area under the curve and acceleration is the gradient; on a displacement–time graph, velocity is the gradient.

Projectile motion results from combining a constant horizontal velocity (no air resistance) with a constant downward acceleration g ≈ 9.81 m s⁻². The horizontal and vertical components are completely independent. Horizontally: x = v_x t. Vertically: y = u_y t − ½gt², v_y = u_y − gt. The trajectory is parabolic. Range is maximised at 45° for launch and landing at the same height, giving R = u² sin 2θ / g.

When fluid resistance (drag) acts on a falling object, the net downward force decreases as speed increases, because drag increases with velocity (F_drag ∝ v or ∝ v² depending on regime). The object accelerates at a rate less than g, and eventually reaches terminal velocity when drag equals weight: mg = kv_T (or kv_T²). At terminal velocity, net force is zero and acceleration is zero; the velocity–time graph levels off to a horizontal asymptote.

At HL, relative velocity is treated algebraically in two dimensions. If observer A moves with velocity v_A and object B has velocity v_B, the velocity of B relative to A is v_BA = v_B − v_A. Vector subtraction using components or scale drawing gives the relative velocity. This extends to problems such as river crossings and rain at an angle. Graphical methods — including vector triangles — are essential tools for two-dimensional kinematics problems.

quiz.question: Define displacement and explain how it differs from distance.
quiz.markscheme: Displacement is a vector quantity representing the change in position from a starting point to an end point, including both magnitude and direction. Distance is a scalar quantity representing the total path length travelled, regardless of direction. For example, an object moving 3 m east then 4 m north has a distance of 7 m but a displacement of 5 m (since √(3² + 4²) = 5 m) at an angle north of east.

quiz.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest and reaches a velocity of 20 m s⁻¹ in 8.0 s. Calculate the acceleration and the distance travelled.
quiz.markscheme: Acceleration: a = (v − u)/t = (20 − 0)/8.0 = 2.5 m s⁻². Distance: s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½(2.5)(8.0²) = ½ × 2.5 × 64 = 80 m. Alternatively, v² = u² + 2as → 400 = 2(2.5)s → s = 80 m. Or s = ½(u + v)t = ½(0 + 20)(8) = 80 m.

quiz.question: Describe the motion of a projectile launched horizontally from a cliff of height 45 m at 15 m s⁻¹. Calculate the time of flight and the horizontal range. (g = 9.81 m s⁻²)
quiz.markscheme: The projectile has constant horizontal velocity 15 m s⁻¹ and constant downward acceleration 9.81 m s⁻². Vertically: h = ½gt² → 45 = ½(9.81)t² → t² = 9.17 → t = 3.03 s. Horizontally: R = v_x × t = 15 × 3.03 = 45.4 m ≈ 45 m. At all times, horizontal and vertical components are independent.

quiz.question: Explain the concept of terminal velocity for an object falling through air, and sketch the velocity–time graph.
quiz.markscheme: As the object falls, gravitational force (mg) is constant downward. Air resistance increases with speed. Initially acceleration ≈ g; as speed increases, drag increases and net force decreases, so acceleration decreases. Eventually drag = mg, net force = 0, acceleration = 0, and the object moves at constant terminal velocity v_T. The v–t graph starts with gradient g, curves upward with decreasing gradient, and asymptotically approaches the terminal velocity as a horizontal line.

quiz.question: A velocity–time graph shows a straight line from (0, 4) to (6, 16). Determine the acceleration and the displacement over this 6 s interval.
quiz.markscheme: Acceleration = gradient = (v₂ − v₁)/(t₂ − t₁) = (16 − 4)/(6 − 0) = 12/6 = 2.0 m s⁻². Displacement = area under graph = area of trapezium = ½(v₁ + v₂) × t = ½(4 + 16) × 6 = ½ × 20 × 6 = 60 m.

quiz.question: A boat can travel at 4.0 m s⁻¹ relative to the water. A river flows at 3.0 m s⁻¹. If the boat points perpendicular to the bank, calculate the resultant velocity and the angle it makes with the bank. (HL)
quiz.markscheme: The resultant velocity is v = √(v_boat² + v_river²) = √(4.0² + 3.0²) = √(16 + 9) = √25 = 5.0 m s⁻¹. The angle to the bank (direction of river flow): tan θ = v_boat/v_river = 4.0/3.0, so θ = arctan(1.33) ≈ 53° from the bank (i.e. the boat moves 53° upstream from the bank direction). The angle to the bank on the downstream side is 90° − 53° = 37°.

title: Forces and momentum
objectives: State Newton's three laws of motion and apply them to systems in equilibrium and in acceleration.
Draw and interpret free-body diagrams, resolving forces into components along chosen axes.
Define linear momentum as p = mv and impulse as J = FΔt, and apply the impulse–momentum theorem.
Apply the law of conservation of momentum to elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions.
Explain the origin of viscosity and buoyancy forces, and describe their effects on motion.
Apply Newton's laws to uniform circular motion, identifying centripetal force and acceleration (a = v²/r).
Analyse angular velocity, centripetal acceleration, and vertical circular motion at HL.
summary:
Newton's first law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net external force. Newton's second law relates net force to the rate of change of momentum: F_net = ma (when mass is constant), or more generally F_net = Δp/Δt. Newton's third law states that when body A exerts a force on body B, body B exerts an equal and opposite force on body A — these action–reaction pairs act on different bodies.

A free-body diagram isolates one object and shows all forces acting on it as vectors with tails at the object. Common forces include weight (W = mg downward), normal reaction (perpendicular to surface), friction (f ≤ μN, opposing relative motion), tension, and applied forces. Resolving forces along perpendicular axes (often parallel and perpendicular to a slope) simplifies calculations: ΣF_x = ma_x and ΣF_y = ma_y.

Linear momentum p = mv (kg m s⁻¹) is a vector. The impulse of a force J = FΔt = Δp, which means a large force over a short time (or a smaller force over longer time) produces the same change in momentum. The area under a force–time graph equals the impulse. The law of conservation of momentum states that in a closed system (no net external force) the total momentum is constant: Σp_before = Σp_after. In elastic collisions, kinetic energy is also conserved; in perfectly inelastic collisions, objects stick together and kinetic energy is not conserved.

Viscosity is the internal friction of a fluid; it gives rise to a drag force on objects moving through fluids, approximated by Stokes' law for spheres at low speed: F = 6πηrv. Buoyancy (upthrust) arises from the pressure difference between the bottom and top of a submerged object: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g (Archimedes' principle). An object floats when upthrust equals weight.

For uniform circular motion, the object moves at constant speed but continuously changing direction; the acceleration is centripetal (directed towards the centre): a_c = v²/r = ω²r, where ω = 2π/T is the angular velocity. The centripetal force F_c = mv²/r is not a new type of force — it is the resultant of existing forces such as tension, gravity, or friction directed towards the centre. At HL, problems extend to vertical circles where the centripetal force equation must account for the weight component; at the top of a loop F_net = mg + N = mv²/r, and at the bottom F_net = N − mg = mv²/r.

quiz.question: State Newton's second law and explain why it is more accurately expressed as F = Δp/Δt than F = ma.
quiz.markscheme: Newton's second law: the net force acting on an object equals the rate of change of its momentum, F_net = Δp/Δt. The form F = ma is valid only when mass is constant. The more general form F = d(mv)/dt handles variable mass situations (e.g. rockets expelling mass). In both forms, force and acceleration are in the same direction as the net force.

quiz.question: Draw a free-body diagram for a 5.0 kg block on a frictionless 30° incline and calculate the acceleration down the slope.
quiz.markscheme: Forces: weight W = mg = 5.0 × 9.81 = 49.1 N downward; normal reaction N perpendicular to slope. Resolving parallel to slope: net force = mg sin 30° = 49.1 × 0.5 = 24.5 N. Perpendicular: N = mg cos 30° = 42.5 N. Acceleration a = F/m = 24.5/5.0 = 4.9 m s⁻² down the slope.

quiz.question: A 0.15 kg ball strikes a wall with velocity 12 m s⁻¹ and rebounds at 8.0 m s⁻¹. The contact time is 0.020 s. Calculate the impulse and average force exerted by the wall.
quiz.markscheme: Take the initial direction as positive. Change in momentum = m(v_f − v_i) = 0.15 × (−8.0 − 12) = 0.15 × (−20) = −3.0 N s. Impulse magnitude = 3.0 N s directed away from the wall. Average force F = J/Δt = 3.0/0.020 = 150 N, directed away from the wall (i.e. the wall pushes the ball back).

quiz.question: A 2.0 kg trolley moving at 4.0 m s⁻¹ collides with a stationary 3.0 kg trolley and they stick together. Show that momentum is conserved and calculate the loss of kinetic energy.
quiz.markscheme: Initial momentum: p_i = 2.0 × 4.0 + 3.0 × 0 = 8.0 kg m s⁻¹. After collision: v_f = p_i/(m₁ + m₂) = 8.0/5.0 = 1.6 m s⁻¹. Final momentum = 5.0 × 1.6 = 8.0 kg m s⁻¹ — conserved. Initial KE = ½(2.0)(4.0²) = 16 J. Final KE = ½(5.0)(1.6²) = 6.4 J. Loss = 16 − 6.4 = 9.6 J (converted to heat and sound).

quiz.question: Explain why a ship floats, using Archimedes' principle.
quiz.markscheme: Archimedes' principle states that the upthrust on an object equals the weight of fluid displaced: F_b = ρ_fluid V_displaced g. A ship floats when the upthrust equals its total weight. As the ship is loaded it sinks deeper, displacing more water until upthrust again equals weight. The hull shape ensures a large volume is displaced at moderate depth, so even a dense steel hull floats because the average density of the ship (including air inside) is less than that of water.

quiz.question: A car of mass 1200 kg travels around a circular bend of radius 80 m at 15 m s⁻¹. Calculate the centripetal force and identify which force provides it.
quiz.markscheme: Centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r = 15²/80 = 225/80 = 2.81 m s⁻². Centripetal force F_c = ma_c = 1200 × 2.81 = 3375 N ≈ 3400 N directed towards the centre of the circle. This force is provided by the static friction between the tyres and the road. If the road is banked, the horizontal component of the normal reaction also contributes to the centripetal force.

quiz.question: A 0.50 kg ball on a string of length 1.2 m completes a vertical circle. Calculate the minimum speed at the top of the circle to maintain tension in the string. (HL)
quiz.markscheme: At the top of the circle, both tension T and weight mg act downward, providing centripetal force: T + mg = mv²/r. For minimum speed, T = 0, so mg = mv²_min/r → v²_min = gr = 9.81 × 1.2 = 11.77 m² s⁻² → v_min = √11.77 ≈ 3.43 m s⁻¹. Below this speed, the string would go slack and circular motion would not be maintained.

title: Work, energy and power
objectives: Define work as W = Fs cos θ and apply it to calculate work done by constant and variable forces.
State the work–energy theorem and use it to relate net work to changes in kinetic energy.
Define gravitational potential energy (E_p = mgh) and elastic potential energy (E_p = ½kx²) and apply conservation of energy.
Define power as P = W/t = Fv and distinguish between useful output power and input power via efficiency.
Describe and compare different energy sources in terms of energy density, renewability, and environmental impact.
Apply Hooke's law (F = kx) and derive the work stored in a spring, extending to non-linear elastic materials at HL.
summary:
Work is done when a force causes displacement. For a constant force F at angle θ to the displacement s, the work done is W = Fs cos θ (joules). Work is a scalar quantity. A force perpendicular to motion does no work (e.g. centripetal force). For a variable force, work equals the area under a force–displacement graph.

The work–energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: W_net = ΔE_k = ½mv² − ½mu². Kinetic energy E_k = ½mv² depends on mass and the square of speed. This theorem is derived directly from Newton's second law combined with the kinematic equation v² = u² + 2as.

Gravitational potential energy near Earth's surface: E_p = mgh, measured relative to a chosen reference level. Elastic potential energy stored in a spring obeying Hooke's law (F = kx, where k is the spring constant in N m⁻¹ and x is the extension): E_p = ½kx². The elastic potential energy equals the area of the triangle under the F–x graph. Conservation of mechanical energy applies when only conservative forces act: E_k + E_p = constant. In real situations, non-conservative forces (friction, drag) dissipate energy as thermal energy and sound, so total energy is still conserved but useful mechanical energy decreases.

Power is the rate of doing work: P = W/t (watts, W = J s⁻¹). For a force F producing velocity v in the same direction, P = Fv. Efficiency η = useful output power / total input power = P_out / P_in, expressed as a fraction or percentage. A Sankey diagram illustrates energy flow showing useful and wasted energy.

Energy sources include fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas — high energy density but non-renewable and releasing CO₂), nuclear fission, and renewables (solar, wind, hydroelectric, tidal, geothermal). Renewable sources have low or zero carbon emissions but variable availability. Solar panels convert solar irradiance (W m⁻²) to electrical energy; wind turbines extract kinetic energy from moving air. At HL, the non-linear force–extension relationship of real materials and calculation of work using integration or area under non-linear graphs extends the analysis of elastic energy.

quiz.question: Calculate the work done when a 50 N force acts on an object at 60° to the direction of displacement of 8.0 m.
quiz.markscheme: W = Fs cos θ = 50 × 8.0 × cos 60° = 400 × 0.5 = 200 J. Only the component of force parallel to the displacement (F cos θ = 50 × 0.5 = 25 N) does work. The perpendicular component does no work.

quiz.question: Apply the work–energy theorem to find the final speed of a 2.0 kg object that starts from rest and has 60 J of net work done on it.
quiz.markscheme: Work–energy theorem: W_net = ΔE_k = ½mv² − ½mu². With u = 0: 60 = ½(2.0)v² → v² = 60 → v = √60 ≈ 7.75 m s⁻¹. This means all the net work goes into kinetic energy, consistent with there being no friction or other dissipative force doing negative work.

quiz.question: A 0.20 kg ball is released from rest at a height of 3.0 m. Calculate its speed just before impact with the ground, using conservation of energy.
quiz.markscheme: Initial E_p = mgh = 0.20 × 9.81 × 3.0 = 5.89 J; E_k = 0. At the ground (reference level), E_p = 0 and all energy is kinetic. E_k = ½mv² = 5.89 J → v² = 2 × 5.89/0.20 = 58.9 → v = √58.9 ≈ 7.67 m s⁻¹. (Alternatively, using v² = 2gh = 2 × 9.81 × 3.0 = 58.9.)

quiz.question: A machine lifts a 500 kg load through 12 m in 30 s. Its efficiency is 60%. Calculate the useful output power and the input power.
quiz.markscheme: Useful work = mgh = 500 × 9.81 × 12 = 58 860 J. Useful output power P_out = W/t = 58 860/30 = 1962 W ≈ 2.0 kW. Efficiency = P_out/P_in → P_in = P_out/efficiency = 1962/0.60 = 3270 W ≈ 3.3 kW. The remaining 40% of input power (≈ 1.3 kW) is wasted as heat in the motor and gears.

quiz.question: Compare fossil fuels and solar energy as sources of electrical power, discussing energy density, renewability, and environmental impact.
quiz.markscheme: Fossil fuels have very high energy density (e.g. coal ≈ 24 MJ kg⁻¹, oil ≈ 42 MJ kg⁻¹) and can generate power on demand (dispatchable). However, they are non-renewable and combustion releases CO₂ (greenhouse gas) and other pollutants. Solar energy is renewable (powered by the Sun) and produces no direct emissions during operation, but has low energy density and is intermittent (dependent on sunlight hours). Solar panels require large surface areas and have lower overall conversion efficiency (~15–20% for photovoltaic). Energy storage (batteries) is needed to address intermittency.

quiz.question: A spring has spring constant k = 400 N m⁻¹. Calculate the elastic potential energy stored when it is compressed by 0.050 m, and determine the force required at that compression.
quiz.markscheme: Hooke's law: F = kx = 400 × 0.050 = 20 N. Elastic PE: E_p = ½kx² = ½ × 400 × (0.050)² = ½ × 400 × 0.0025 = 0.50 J. The energy stored equals the area of the triangle under the F–x graph: ½ × F × x = ½ × 20 × 0.050 = 0.50 J (consistent).

title: Rigid body mechanics
objectives: Define torque (moment of a force) as τ = Fr sin θ and apply the conditions for translational and rotational equilibrium.
Describe the rotational analogues of displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, mass, and momentum.
Apply the equations of rotational kinematics (ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = ω₀t + ½αt²) to problems involving constant angular acceleration.
Define moment of inertia I = Σmr² and use it in Newton's second law for rotation: τ_net = Iα.
Apply conservation of angular momentum L = Iω to systems where no net external torque acts.
Calculate rotational kinetic energy E_rot = ½Iω² and apply the total energy equation for rolling objects.
summary:
Rigid body mechanics extends Newtonian dynamics to objects that can rotate as well as translate. The rotational analogue of force is torque (moment): τ = Fr sin θ, where F is the applied force, r is the distance from the pivot, and θ is the angle between the force and the position vector. Torque has units of N m. For a rigid body in static equilibrium, both the net force and the net torque about any point must be zero: ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0.

The angular kinematic quantities mirror the linear ones: angular displacement θ (rad), angular velocity ω = dθ/dt (rad s⁻¹), and angular acceleration α = dω/dt (rad s⁻²). For uniform angular acceleration, the equations are: ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ; θ = ½(ω₀ + ω)t. Linear and angular quantities are related by v = rω and a_tangential = rα, with centripetal acceleration a_c = ω²r.

The moment of inertia I = Σmᵢrᵢ² (kg m²) is the rotational analogue of mass; it measures resistance to changes in rotational motion. It depends on both the total mass and its distribution about the axis. Common values: solid disk I = ½MR²; hollow ring I = MR²; solid sphere I = ⅖MR²; thin rod about centre I = (1/12)ML². Newton's second law for rotation is τ_net = Iα, analogous to F_net = ma.

Angular momentum L = Iω (kg m² s⁻¹) is the rotational analogue of linear momentum. The law of conservation of angular momentum states that when no net external torque acts, L is constant: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. This explains why a spinning figure skater speeds up when arms are drawn in (I decreases, ω increases). Rotational kinetic energy E_rot = ½Iω². For a rolling object (no slipping), total kinetic energy = ½mv² + ½Iω², where v = rω.

Equilibrium problems require careful choice of the pivot point to eliminate unknown forces from the torque equation. Real-world applications include cranes, bridges, seesaws, and the analysis of the human spine under load. The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² allows the moment of inertia about any axis to be calculated from the moment of inertia about the centre of mass axis.

quiz.question: A uniform horizontal beam of mass 20 kg and length 4.0 m is supported at both ends. A 50 kg person stands 1.5 m from the left support. Calculate the reaction forces at each support.
quiz.markscheme: Let R_L and R_R be the reactions. Taking torques about the left support (clockwise positive): −R_R × 4.0 + (50 × 9.81 × 1.5) + (20 × 9.81 × 2.0) = 0. So R_R × 4.0 = 735.75 + 392.4 = 1128.15 → R_R = 282 N. ΣF_y = 0: R_L + R_R = (50 + 20) × 9.81 = 686.7 N → R_L = 686.7 − 282 = 405 N.

quiz.question: State the rotational analogues of mass, force, velocity, and momentum, including their units.
quiz.markscheme: Mass → moment of inertia I (kg m²). Force → torque τ (N m). Linear velocity v → angular velocity ω (rad s⁻¹). Linear momentum p → angular momentum L (kg m² s⁻¹). Linear kinetic energy ½mv² → rotational kinetic energy ½Iω². Newton's second law F = ma → τ = Iα.

quiz.question: A flywheel (solid disk, mass 5.0 kg, radius 0.30 m) is accelerated from rest to 120 rad s⁻¹ in 8.0 s. Calculate the angular acceleration, torque required, and angle turned.

Calculate the moment of inertia of a 3.0 kg solid sphere of radius 0.20 m and the moment of inertia of a thin ring of the same mass and radius. Compare their resistances to angular acceleration under the same torque.
quiz.markscheme: Solid sphere: I = ⅖MR² = ⅖ × 3.0 × (0.20)² = 0.4 × 3.0 × 0.04 = 0.048 kg m². Thin ring: I = MR² = 3.0 × (0.20)² = 3.0 × 0.04 = 0.12 kg m². Under the same torque τ, α = τ/I. The sphere has a smaller I, so α_sphere = τ/0.048 is larger than α_ring = τ/0.12 — the sphere accelerates more easily because mass is concentrated near the centre.

quiz.question: A figure skater spinning at 2.0 rev s⁻¹ with arms extended (I = 4.5 kg m²) pulls her arms in so I = 1.8 kg m²). Calculate her new angular velocity.
quiz.markscheme: Conservation of angular momentum: L = I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. ω₁ = 2.0 rev s⁻¹ × 2π = 4π rad s⁻¹ ≈ 12.57 rad s⁻¹. I₁ω₁ = 4.5 × 12.57 = 56.5 kg m² s⁻¹. ω₂ = L/I₂ = 56.5/1.8 = 31.4 rad s⁻¹ = 5.0 rev s⁻¹. The spin rate increases by a factor of 2.5 because I decreased by a factor of 2.5.

quiz.question: A solid cylinder (mass 2.0 kg, radius 0.15 m) rolls without slipping down a slope from rest, dropping a height of 0.80 m. Calculate its linear speed at the bottom.
quiz.markscheme: Conservation of energy: mgh = ½mv² + ½Iω². For solid cylinder I = ½mr², and v = rω → ω = v/r. So mgh = ½mv² + ½(½mr²)(v/r)² = ½mv² + ¼mv² = ¾mv². Thus v² = (4/3)gh = (4/3) × 9.81 × 0.80 = 10.45 → v = √10.45 ≈ 3.23 m s⁻¹. If it had slid without rolling: v² = 2gh = 15.7 → v = 3.96 m s⁻¹ (faster, as no energy goes to rotation).

title: Galilean and special relativity
objectives: Describe Galilean relativity and explain why it fails to account for the constancy of the speed of light.
State Einstein's two postulates of special relativity and explain their implications for absolute time and space.
Apply the Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²) to problems involving time dilation (t = γt₀) and length contraction (L = L₀/γ).
Explain the concept of simultaneity and how observers in different inertial frames disagree on whether events are simultaneous.
Apply the relativistic velocity addition formula and discuss invariant quantities such as the spacetime interval.
Interpret spacetime diagrams (Minkowski diagrams) showing world lines, light cones, and the relationship between events.
summary:
Galilean relativity, established by Newton's mechanics, holds that the laws of mechanics are the same in all inertial (non-accelerating) reference frames. The Galilean velocity transformation v' = v − u states that velocities simply add. However, Maxwell's equations of electromagnetism predict that light travels at c ≈ 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ regardless of the motion of the source. The Michelson–Morley experiment (1887) found no variation in the speed of light with direction, contradicting Galilean expectations and undermining the notion of a luminiferous ether.

Einstein's two postulates of special relativity (1905): (1) The laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames. (2) The speed of light in a vacuum c is the same for all inertial observers, regardless of the motion of the source or observer. These postulates require abandoning the concepts of absolute time and absolute space.

The Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²) ≥ 1, where v is the relative speed between frames and β = v/c. Time dilation: a moving clock runs slow — the proper time t₀ measured in the rest frame of the clock is related to the dilated time t measured in another frame by t = γt₀. Length contraction: an object moving relative to an observer appears shorter along the direction of motion — proper length L₀ (measured at rest) is related to the contracted length L = L₀/γ. Both effects are reciprocal and real, not illusions.

Simultaneity is relative: two events that are simultaneous in one inertial frame are generally not simultaneous in another. The relativity of simultaneity follows directly from the second postulate. Relativistic velocity addition for speeds along one axis: v_rel = (v₁ + v₂)/(1 + v₁v₂/c²), ensuring that no object exceeds c. The spacetime interval s² = c²t² − x² is invariant (the same value in all inertial frames), even though space and time intervals individually are not.

A Minkowski spacetime diagram has the time axis ct vertical and the space axis x horizontal. Each object traces a world line; stationary objects have vertical world lines and light travels at 45°. Events in the light cone of event O can be causally connected to O; events outside the light cone are space-like separated and cannot influence O. Proper time is the time read by a clock along its world line: Δτ = Δs/c.

quiz.question: Explain why Galilean relativity is inconsistent with the observed constancy of the speed of light.
quiz.markscheme: Galilean relativity gives velocity transformation v' = v − u: an observer moving at speed u relative to a light source should measure light at speed c − u. Experiments (Michelson–Morley) show the speed of light is c for all observers regardless of their motion. This contradicts the Galilean transformation, which assumes time is absolute and universal. Einstein resolved this by postulating that c is the same in all inertial frames, requiring new transformation equations (Lorentz transformations) and the abandonment of absolute time.

quiz.question: State Einstein's two postulates and explain what they imply about measurements of time and length for observers in relative motion.
quiz.markscheme: Postulate 1: the laws of physics are identical in all inertial reference frames. Postulate 2: the speed of light in a vacuum is c for all inertial observers, independent of the motion of the source. Consequences: (a) time dilation — a moving clock appears to run slow (t = γt₀); (b) length contraction — a moving object appears shorter (L = L₀/γ); (c) simultaneity is relative — events simultaneous in one frame may not be in another. These effects become significant only when v is an appreciable fraction of c.

quiz.question: A muon is created in the upper atmosphere at a height of 15 km above Earth's surface and travels downward at v = 0.98c. Calculate the Lorentz factor, the muon's proper lifetime if its dilated lifetime is 34 μs, and the contracted height from the muon's frame.
quiz.markscheme: γ = 1/√(1 − 0.98²) = 1/√(1 − 0.9604) = 1/√0.0396 = 1/0.199 ≈ 5.03. Proper lifetime: t₀ = t/γ = 34/5.03 ≈ 6.76 μs ≈ 6.8 μs (consistent with measured muon lifetime ~2.2 μs in some versions). Contracted height: L = L₀/γ = 15 000/5.03 ≈ 2983 m ≈ 3.0 km. From the muon's frame, the atmosphere is compressed to ~3 km, so the muon can survive the journey in its own proper time.

quiz.question: Explain the relativity of simultaneity using a thought experiment involving a train and lightning strikes.
quiz.markscheme: Imagine a train moving at speed v. Two lightning bolts strike both ends of the train simultaneously in the ground frame — an observer at the midpoint of the track sees both flashes at the same time. A passenger at the centre of the train is moving towards the front strike and away from the rear strike. Because light travels at c in both frames, the passenger receives the front flash before the rear flash, and concludes the front was struck first. Both observers are correct in their own frames. This demonstrates that simultaneity is not absolute — it depends on the reference frame of the observer.

quiz.question: Spacecraft A travels at 0.70c relative to Earth. Spacecraft B travels in the same direction at 0.80c relative to Earth. Calculate the speed of B as measured by A, using relativistic velocity addition.
quiz.markscheme: Relativistic addition: v_rel = (v_B − v_A)/(1 − v_A v_B/c²) = (0.80c − 0.70c)/(1 − 0.70 × 0.80) = 0.10c/(1 − 0.56) = 0.10c/0.44 = 0.227c ≈ 0.23c. Galilean addition would give 0.10c; relativistic addition gives a slightly different result but the key point is that v_rel < c (in fact, even if both were 0.99c, the result would still be less than c).

quiz.question: Describe the features of a Minkowski spacetime diagram and explain how time dilation can be read from the diagram.
quiz.markscheme: A Minkowski diagram has axes ct (vertical) and x (horizontal). A stationary observer has a vertical world line (x = constant). A moving observer at speed v has a tilted world line with gradient c/v (since slope = Δ(ct)/Δx = c/v). Light rays travel at 45° (slope = 1). The proper time along a world line is shorter than the coordinate time (time dilation): τ = t/γ. Graphically, equal intervals of proper time along the moving observer's tilted world line correspond to larger coordinate time intervals. The invariant spacetime interval s² = c²t² − x² is represented by hyperbolas; all frames agree on the value of s².

title: Thermal energy transfers
objectives: Define temperature, internal energy, and thermal energy, distinguishing between heat and temperature.
Apply the specific heat capacity equation Q = mcΔT to calculate energy exchanges during heating and cooling.
Explain the concept of latent heat and apply Q = mL to phase changes at constant temperature.
Describe the three mechanisms of thermal energy transfer: conduction, convection, and radiation.
Apply the Stefan–Boltzmann law (P = σAT⁴) and Wien's displacement law (λ_max T = 2.90 × 10⁻³ m K) to stellar and blackbody radiation.
Define luminosity and apparent brightness, and apply the inverse square law for light intensity.
summary:
Temperature is a measure of the average random kinetic energy of the particles in a substance; it is measured in kelvin (T/K = T/°C + 273). Internal energy is the total energy of all particles in a substance — both their random kinetic energy and their potential energy due to intermolecular forces. Thermal energy (heat) Q is energy transferred from a hotter body to a cooler one due to a temperature difference; it is a process, not a property.

The specific heat capacity c of a substance is the energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 K: Q = mcΔT (joules). For water, c ≈ 4200 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹. When two objects reach thermal equilibrium, heat gained by the cooler body equals heat lost by the hotter body (assuming no losses to surroundings).

A phase change (melting, boiling) occurs at constant temperature despite energy being supplied. This energy breaks intermolecular bonds rather than raising kinetic energy. The specific latent heat L is defined by Q = mL. Latent heat of fusion L_f refers to the solid–liquid transition; latent heat of vaporisation L_v refers to the liquid–gas transition (L_v > L_f for most substances because gas molecules must be fully separated).

Conduction is heat transfer through a solid by direct particle collisions and, in metals, by free electrons. Fourier's law of conduction: Q/t = kA(ΔT/d), where k is thermal conductivity, A is cross-sectional area, and d is thickness. Convection is heat transfer through a fluid by the bulk movement of warmer, less dense fluid rising and cooler fluid sinking. Radiation is the transfer of energy by electromagnetic waves (principally infrared) and requires no medium.

A perfect blackbody absorbs all incident radiation and emits radiation according to the Stefan–Boltzmann law: P = σAT⁴, where σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant and T is absolute temperature. Wien's displacement law gives the peak wavelength of emission: λ_max T = 2.90 × 10⁻³ m K — hotter objects emit at shorter wavelengths (bluer). Stars are approximated as blackbodies; their colour indicates surface temperature. Luminosity L = σAT⁴ × 4πR² for a spherical star of radius R. Apparent brightness b = L/(4πd²) — the inverse square law — where d is the distance to the star.

quiz.question: Distinguish between heat and temperature, and explain why stirring hot coffee does not change its temperature significantly.
quiz.markscheme: Temperature measures the average random kinetic energy per particle (in kelvin or Celsius). Heat is energy transferred between bodies due to a temperature difference; it is a process, not a stored quantity. Stirring transfers mechanical (ordered) energy to the coffee, slightly increasing internal energy, but the rise is negligible because the thermal capacity of the coffee is large compared to the work done by stirring. Temperature reflects random molecular kinetic energy, not ordered mechanical motion.

quiz.question: Calculate the energy needed to raise 2.0 kg of water from 20°C to 100°C, and then to vaporise it completely. (c_water = 4200 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹, L_v = 2.26 × 10⁶ J kg⁻¹)
quiz.markscheme: Heating: Q₁ = mcΔT = 2.0 × 4200 × (100 − 20) = 2.0 × 4200 × 80 = 672 000 J = 672 kJ. Vaporisation: Q₂ = mL_v = 2.0 × 2.26 × 10⁶ = 4.52 × 10⁶ J = 4520 kJ. Total = 672 + 4520 = 5192 kJ ≈ 5.2 MJ. Note that much more energy is needed for vaporisation than for heating: the gas phase requires complete separation of intermolecular bonds.

quiz.question: Describe the difference between conduction and convection as mechanisms of thermal energy transfer, with one example of each.
quiz.markscheme: Conduction: energy transferred through a material by direct molecular collisions (and free electrons in metals) without bulk movement of material. Example: heat spreading along a metal rod when one end is heated. Convection: energy transferred by bulk movement of a fluid — heated fluid expands, becomes less dense, and rises; cooler fluid sinks to replace it, creating convection currents. Example: a radiator heating a room — warm air rises, cool air at the floor moves toward the radiator, circulates. Conduction occurs in solids; convection in fluids (liquids and gases).

quiz.question: A star has surface temperature 6000 K and radius 1.5 times the Sun's radius (R_Sun = 6.96 × 10⁸ m). Calculate its luminosity. (σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴)
Apply Wien's displacement law to determine the surface temperature of a star whose peak emission wavelength is 480 nm.
quiz.markscheme: Wien's law: λ_max T = 2.90 × 10⁻³ m K. T = (2.90 × 10⁻³)/(λ_max) = (2.90 × 10⁻³)/(480 × 10⁻⁹) = (2.90 × 10⁻³)/(4.80 × 10⁻⁷) = 6042 K ≈ 6000 K. A peak wavelength in the blue-green visible range corresponds to a hot star — blue stars have higher surface temperatures than red stars, which have longer peak wavelengths.

quiz.question: A star has apparent brightness 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ W m⁻² at Earth. If its luminosity is 4.0 × 10²⁶ W, calculate its distance from Earth.
quiz.markscheme: Inverse square law: b = L/(4πd²) → d² = L/(4πb) = (4.0 × 10²⁶)/(4π × 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) = (4.0 × 10²⁶)/(4.02 × 10⁻⁹) = 9.95 × 10³⁴ m². d = √(9.95 × 10³⁴) ≈ 3.15 × 10¹⁷ m ≈ 3.2 × 10¹⁷ m (about 10 parsecs or ~33 light-years).

title: Greenhouse effect
objectives: Describe the mechanism of the greenhouse effect and distinguish between natural and enhanced greenhouse effects.
Explain why certain gases (CO₂, CH₄, H₂O, N₂O) are greenhouse gases in terms of molecular vibration and IR absorption.
Apply the concept of albedo and calculate the equilibrium temperature of a planet using the Stefan–Boltzmann law.
Discuss the evidence for and consequences of enhanced global warming, including feedback mechanisms.
Evaluate strategies for reducing greenhouse gas emissions, including renewable energy, carbon capture, and international agreements.
summary:
Solar radiation peaks in the visible range (~500 nm) and passes through the atmosphere largely unhindered (the atmosphere is transparent to visible light). Earth's surface absorbs this radiation and re-emits it as infrared (IR) radiation at longer wavelengths (peak ~10 μm) due to its lower temperature (~255–288 K). Greenhouse gases (GHGs) — principally CO₂, CH₄, H₂O vapour, and N₂O — absorb and re-emit this outgoing IR radiation, trapping energy in the lower atmosphere. This is the greenhouse effect; without it, Earth's mean surface temperature would be approximately −18°C instead of +15°C.

Greenhouse gases absorb IR because their molecular bonds undergo resonant vibrational modes at the same frequencies as the incoming IR photons. Diatomic symmetric molecules (N₂, O₂) do not absorb IR because they lack an oscillating electric dipole moment. Polyatomic asymmetric molecules (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄) have asymmetric stretching and bending modes that couple with IR.

The equilibrium temperature of a planet can be estimated by balancing absorbed solar power with emitted blackbody power. Solar power absorbed: P_absorbed = (1 − α) × S × πR², where α is the albedo (fraction of incident radiation reflected), S is the solar constant (≈ 1361 W m⁻²), and R is the planet radius. Power emitted: P_emitted = σ × 4πR² × T⁴. Setting P_absorbed = P_emitted gives T_e = [S(1−α)/(4σ)]^(1/4). For Earth (α ≈ 0.30), T_e ≈ 255 K (−18°C). The actual surface temperature (~288 K) is higher due to the greenhouse effect.

The enhanced greenhouse effect results from human activities increasing atmospheric concentrations of GHGs, primarily CO₂ from fossil fuel combustion and deforestation, and CH₄ from agriculture and landfill. Evidence includes rising CO₂ levels (exceeding 420 ppm), rising global average temperatures, sea level rise, and melting ice caps. Feedback mechanisms include: (positive) melting of ice reduces albedo, permafrost thaw releases CH₄; (negative) increased plant growth absorbs more CO₂. Strategies include transitioning to renewable energy, improving energy efficiency, carbon capture and storage (CCS), reforestation, and international frameworks such as the Paris Agreement.

quiz.question: Explain the natural greenhouse effect and describe how it keeps Earth's surface warmer than would otherwise be expected.
quiz.markscheme: Solar radiation (mostly visible) passes through the atmosphere and is absorbed by Earth's surface, warming it. The surface re-emits longer-wavelength infrared (IR) radiation. Greenhouse gases (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, N₂O) in the atmosphere absorb and re-emit this IR in all directions, including back toward Earth, trapping energy in the lower atmosphere. This raises the surface temperature above the simple blackbody equilibrium value of −18°C to the actual average of ~+15°C. Without the natural greenhouse effect, Earth would be frozen.

quiz.question: Explain why CO₂ is a greenhouse gas but N₂ is not, in terms of molecular structure and IR absorption.
quiz.markscheme: CO₂ is a triatomic molecule with asymmetric vibrational modes (bending and asymmetric stretching) that produce an oscillating electric dipole moment. These modes have natural frequencies matching IR radiation frequencies (~15 μm for bending), so CO₂ absorbs IR photons resonantly. N₂ is a symmetric diatomic molecule; its bond vibrations do not create a changing dipole moment, so it cannot absorb IR radiation. Similarly, O₂ does not absorb IR. Only molecules with changing electric dipole moments during vibration act as greenhouse gases.

quiz.question: Calculate the equilibrium temperature of a planet with albedo 0.35, given solar constant S = 1361 W m⁻² and σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴.
quiz.markscheme: Balancing power: S(1−α)πR² = σ(4πR²)T⁴. Simplifying: T⁴ = S(1−α)/(4σ) = 1361 × 0.65/(4 × 5.67 × 10⁻⁸) = 884.65/(2.268 × 10⁻⁷) = 3.901 × 10⁹ K⁴. T = (3.901 × 10⁹)^(1/4) = (3.901)^(1/4) × 10^(9/4) = 1.404 × 10^2.25 = 1.404 × 177.8 ≈ 249.7 K ≈ 250 K. Higher albedo reflects more radiation, resulting in a cooler equilibrium temperature.

quiz.question: Describe two positive feedback mechanisms associated with global warming and explain why they accelerate climate change.
quiz.markscheme: 1. Ice-albedo feedback: as ice melts due to warming, the highly reflective ice surface is replaced by darker ocean or land. The lower albedo means more solar radiation is absorbed, causing further warming, which melts more ice — a self-amplifying cycle. 2. Permafrost–methane feedback: warming thaws Arctic permafrost, releasing stored organic carbon as CO₂ and CH₄ (potent GHGs) into the atmosphere. These gases enhance the greenhouse effect, causing more warming, which thaws more permafrost. Both are positive feedbacks — they amplify the initial warming signal.

quiz.question: Evaluate two strategies for reducing the enhanced greenhouse effect, discussing advantages and limitations of each.
quiz.markscheme: 1. Renewable energy (e.g. solar, wind): replaces fossil fuels, producing electricity with near-zero CO₂ emissions. Advantages: abundant resource, rapidly falling costs, scalable. Limitations: intermittent supply (dependent on sun/wind), requires energy storage and grid upgrades, initial manufacturing has carbon cost. 2. Carbon capture and storage (CCS): captures CO₂ from power station flue gases and stores it underground. Advantages: allows continued use of existing infrastructure, can remove CO₂ from atmosphere. Limitations: high cost (~$50–100/tonne CO₂), unproven at large scale, storage sites may leak, high energy penalty (reduces power plant efficiency by ~20–30%).

title: Gas laws
objectives: State and apply Boyle's law (pV = constant at constant T), Charles's law (V/T = constant at constant p), and Gay-Lussac's law (p/T = constant at constant V).
Derive and apply the combined ideal gas law pV/T = constant and the equation of state pV = nRT.
Describe the molecular model of an ideal gas and the assumptions underlying the kinetic theory.
Derive expressions for the pressure of an ideal gas and relate mean kinetic energy to temperature: E_k = (3/2)k_B T.
Distinguish between ideal and real gases, explaining deviations from ideal behaviour at high pressure and low temperature.
summary:
The three empirical gas laws describe the behaviour of ideal gases. Boyle's law: at constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional: p₁V₁ = p₂V₂ (pV = constant). Charles's law: at constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. Gay-Lussac's law (pressure law): at constant volume, pressure is directly proportional to absolute temperature: p₁/T₁ = p₂/T₂. All three are combined in the ideal gas law: pV/T = constant for a fixed amount of gas.

The equation of state for an ideal gas is pV = nRT, where n is the number of moles of gas (mol), R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ is the universal gas constant, and T is the absolute temperature in kelvin. An equivalent form uses the number of molecules N: pV = Nk_B T, where k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J K⁻¹ is the Boltzmann constant and k_B = R/N_A (N_A = 6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹ is Avogadro's number).

Kinetic theory models a gas as a large number of identical point particles in constant random motion, with no intermolecular forces except during perfectly elastic collisions, and negligible particle volume compared to the container. From kinetic theory, the pressure of an ideal gas is p = (1/3)(Nm/V)⟨c²⟩, where m is particle mass and ⟨c²⟩ is the mean square speed. Comparing with pV = Nk_B T gives the mean kinetic energy of a molecule: E_k = ½m⟨c²⟩ = (3/2)k_B T. Temperature is thus a direct measure of the mean translational kinetic energy per molecule. The root mean square speed is c_rms = √(3k_B T/m) = √(3RT/M), where M is the molar mass.

Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour because (1) molecules have finite volume and (2) intermolecular attractions exist. At high pressure, the finite molecular volume means the actual volume is larger than predicted by pV = nRT. At low temperature or high pressure, intermolecular attractions reduce the kinetic energy available, causing the gas to occupy less volume than predicted. These deviations are described by the van der Waals equation: (p + a n²/V²)(V − nb) = nRT, where a accounts for intermolecular attractions and b for finite molecular volume. At high temperature and low pressure, all real gases approach ideal behaviour.

quiz.question: A gas has volume 2.0 L at pressure 150 kPa. If the temperature is kept constant and the pressure is increased to 450 kPa, calculate the new volume.
quiz.markscheme: Boyle's law: p₁V₁ = p₂V₂ (constant T). V₂ = p₁V₁/p₂ = (150 × 2.0)/450 = 300/450 = 0.667 L ≈ 0.67 L. Tripling the pressure (at constant temperature) reduces the volume to one-third. Note: pressure and volume are always in consistent units, but since we are taking a ratio, any consistent unit works.

quiz.question: A gas occupies 4.0 L at 27°C. Calculate the volume at 127°C at constant pressure.
quiz.markscheme: Convert to Kelvin: T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K; T₂ = 127 + 273 = 400 K. Charles's law: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ → V₂ = V₁ × T₂/T₁ = 4.0 × (400/300) = 4.0 × 1.333 = 5.33 L ≈ 5.3 L. The temperature in Kelvin increases by a factor of 4/3, so the volume also increases by 4/3. Celsius temperatures must be converted to Kelvin before applying gas laws.

quiz.question: State the assumptions of the kinetic model of an ideal gas and explain which assumption breaks down for a real gas at high pressure.
quiz.markscheme: Assumptions: (1) gas consists of a large number of identical point particles with negligible volume; (2) particles are in constant random motion; (3) collisions between particles and with walls are perfectly elastic; (4) there are no intermolecular forces except during collisions; (5) the time of collision is negligible compared to time between collisions. At high pressure, the volume of the molecules themselves becomes significant compared to the total container volume (assumption 1 fails), and the distance between molecules is small enough for intermolecular attractions to be significant (assumption 4 fails).

quiz.question: Calculate the rms speed of nitrogen molecules (M = 0.028 kg mol⁻¹) at 300 K. (R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹)
quiz.markscheme: c_rms = √(3RT/M) = √(3 × 8.314 × 300 / 0.028) = √(7482.6/0.028) = √(267 236) = 517 m s⁻¹ ≈ 520 m s⁻¹. This is approximately the average molecular speed in air at room temperature (for N₂). Heavier molecules at the same temperature move slower (c_rms ∝ 1/√M), and speed increases with temperature (c_rms ∝ √T).

quiz.question: Explain how the kinetic theory leads to the relationship E_k = (3/2)k_B T, and state what this implies about absolute zero.
quiz.markscheme: From kinetic theory, pressure p = (1/3)(Nm/V)⟨c²⟩. From the ideal gas law, pV = Nk_B T. Equating: (1/3)Nm⟨c²⟩ = Nk_B T → ½m⟨c²⟩ = (3/2)k_B T. The left side is the mean translational kinetic energy per molecule. So E_k = (3/2)k_B T. At absolute zero (T = 0 K), E_k = 0, meaning particles would have no thermal kinetic energy. This is the theoretical lower limit of temperature — no substance can be cooled below 0 K.

title: Thermodynamics
objectives: Define thermodynamic system, surroundings, and the concepts of internal energy, work, and heat in the context of thermodynamics.
State and apply the first law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q + W (or ΔU = Q − W depending on sign convention).
Calculate work done by or on a gas during isothermal, adiabatic, isochoric, and isobaric processes on p–V diagrams.
State the second law of thermodynamics and define entropy, explaining why entropy increases in spontaneous processes.
Analyse ideal heat engines using the Carnot cycle and calculate thermal efficiency: η = 1 − T_C/T_H.
Discuss the difference between heat engines and heat pumps, and apply the coefficient of performance (COP).
summary:
Thermodynamics describes energy transformations involving heat, work, and internal energy. A thermodynamic system is the object or region of interest; the surroundings are everything else. Internal energy U is the total microscopic energy of the system. Heat Q is energy transferred due to a temperature difference; work W is energy transferred by a mechanical process (e.g. compression). The sign convention most common in IB Physics: Q > 0 when heat enters the system; W > 0 when work is done on the system (so ΔU = Q + W).

The first law of thermodynamics is a statement of energy conservation: ΔU = Q + W (or ΔU = Q − W when W is work done by the gas). For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature: ΔU = (3/2)nRΔT for a monatomic ideal gas. Work done by a gas against a constant external pressure: W = pΔV (or on a p–V diagram, work = area under the curve). Four special processes: isothermal (ΔT = 0, ΔU = 0, Q = −W); adiabatic (Q = 0, ΔU = W); isochoric (ΔV = 0, W = 0, ΔU = Q); isobaric (constant p, W = pΔV).

The second law of thermodynamics: the total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; for irreversible processes it increases. Entropy S is a state function: ΔS = Q/T for a reversible isothermal process. The second law means heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body, and no heat engine can be 100% efficient.

A heat engine absorbs heat Q_H from a hot reservoir at T_H, performs work W, and rejects heat Q_C to a cold reservoir at T_C. Efficiency η = W/Q_H = 1 − Q_C/Q_H. The Carnot cycle (two isothermal and two adiabatic processes) is the most efficient possible cycle operating between T_H and T_C: η_Carnot = 1 − T_C/T_H. No real engine can exceed Carnot efficiency. A heat pump does work to transfer heat from a cold reservoir to a hot one (e.g. refrigerators, air conditioners). The coefficient of performance COP = Q_H/W = T_H/(T_H − T_C) for heating, or COP = Q_C/W = T_C/(T_H − T_C) for cooling.

quiz.question: State the first law of thermodynamics and apply it to an ideal gas that absorbs 500 J of heat while having 200 J of work done on it.
quiz.markscheme: First law: ΔU = Q + W (where W is work done on the system). Q = +500 J (heat absorbed by system), W = +200 J (work done on system). ΔU = 500 + 200 = 700 J. The internal energy of the gas increases by 700 J. For an ideal gas, this corresponds to a rise in temperature: ΔT = ΔU/(nC_v), where C_v = (3/2)R for a monatomic gas.

quiz.question: Calculate the work done by a gas that expands at constant pressure of 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa from 3.0 L to 7.5 L. Identify this process as isobaric and state the relationship between heat supplied, internal energy change, and work done.
quiz.markscheme: Work done by gas: W = pΔV = 2.0 × 10⁵ × (7.5 − 3.0) × 10⁻³ = 2.0 × 10⁵ × 4.5 × 10⁻³ = 900 J. This is an isobaric (constant pressure) process. By first law: Q = ΔU + W_by_gas (work done by gas). Some of the heat supplied goes to increasing internal energy (raising temperature) and some goes to doing work against the external pressure. On a p–V diagram, the work equals the rectangular area under the horizontal isobar.

quiz.question: Explain the second law of thermodynamics in terms of entropy, and give one example of an irreversible process that illustrates entropy increase.
quiz.markscheme: The second law states that in any natural (spontaneous, irreversible) process, the total entropy of the universe increases: ΔS_total ≥ 0. Entropy is a measure of disorder or the number of accessible microstates. Example: heat conduction from a hot cup of tea to a cold room. The hot object loses entropy ΔS = −Q/T_hot, and the room gains ΔS = +Q/T_cold. Since T_cold < T_hot, the gain exceeds the loss, so total ΔS > 0. The reverse — spontaneous heat flow from cold to hot — never happens because it would decrease total entropy.

quiz.question: A Carnot heat engine operates between 600 K and 300 K. It absorbs 1200 J per cycle from the hot reservoir. Calculate the work output, heat rejected, and thermal efficiency.
quiz.markscheme: Efficiency: η = 1 − T_C/T_H = 1 − 300/600 = 0.50 = 50%. Work output: W = η × Q_H = 0.50 × 1200 = 600 J. Heat rejected: Q_C = Q_H − W = 1200 − 600 = 600 J. The Carnot cycle is the theoretical maximum efficiency — no real engine operating between these temperatures can achieve more than 50% efficiency. The 50% result arises because T_C = T_H/2.

quiz.question: Describe how a heat pump operates and calculate the COP for heating a building if T_H = 293 K and T_C = 273 K.
quiz.markscheme: A heat pump uses work W to transfer heat Q_C from a cold reservoir (outdoor air at T_C) and delivers a larger amount of heat Q_H = Q_C + W to the hot reservoir (building interior at T_H). COP for heating = Q_H/W = T_H/(T_H − T_C) = 293/(293 − 273) = 293/20 = 14.65 ≈ 15. This means for every 1 J of electrical work done, the heat pump delivers ~15 J of heat to the building — far more efficient than a simple electric heater (COP = 1). High COP values are achievable when the temperature difference is small.

quiz.question: Sketch a p–V diagram for a Carnot cycle and identify the four processes. Explain which two processes involve entropy changes and which do not.
quiz.markscheme: The Carnot cycle consists of: (1) isothermal expansion at T_H — gas absorbs Q_H from hot reservoir at constant temperature; (2) adiabatic expansion — gas expands without heat exchange (Q = 0), temperature drops from T_H to T_C; (3) isothermal compression at T_C — gas rejects Q_C to cold reservoir; (4) adiabatic compression — Q = 0, temperature rises from T_C back to T_H. Entropy changes: processes 1 and 3 are isothermal; ΔS = Q/T ≠ 0 (entropy increases in 1, decreases in 3). Adiabatic processes 2 and 4 are reversible with Q = 0, so ΔS = 0 — they are isentropic. For the complete cycle ΔS_cycle = 0, consistent with the Carnot cycle being reversible.

title: Current and circuits
objectives: Define electric current (I = ΔQ/Δt), potential difference (V = W/Q), and resistance (R = V/I), and state Ohm's law.
Apply Kirchhoff's current law (ΣI_in = ΣI_out) and Kirchhoff's voltage law (ΣV = 0) to analyse series and parallel circuits.
Calculate power dissipation in resistors using P = IV = I²R = V²/R and apply conservation of energy in circuits.
Describe the behaviour of capacitors in DC circuits, and apply Q = CV and E = ½CV² to charging and discharging.
Explain the concept of internal resistance (ε = V + Ir) and determine terminal voltage and efficiency of a cell.
Analyse resistor combinations, potential dividers, and sensor circuits (thermistor, LDR) in both SL and HL contexts.
summary:
Electric current I = ΔQ/Δt (amperes) is the rate of flow of charge. In conductors, current is carried by free electrons flowing from lower to higher potential (conventional current flows from + to −). Potential difference (voltage) V = W/Q is the work done per unit charge between two points. Resistance R = V/I (ohms, Ω). Ohm's law states that for a metallic conductor at constant temperature, V ∝ I, so R is constant. Non-ohmic devices (diodes, thermistors, filament bulbs) have R that varies with current or temperature.

Resistors in series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... (same current through each). Resistors in parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ... (same voltage across each). Kirchhoff's current law (KCL): at any junction, the sum of currents entering equals the sum leaving (conservation of charge). Kirchhoff's voltage law (KVL): the sum of potential differences around any closed loop is zero (conservation of energy).

Electrical power P = IV = I²R = V²/R. The energy dissipated (as heat) in a resistor: E = Pt = IVt. A potential divider uses two resistors in series to produce a fraction of the supply voltage: V_out = V_in × R₂/(R₁ + R₂). Thermistors (resistance decreases with temperature) and LDRs (resistance decreases with light intensity) are used as sensors in potential divider circuits.

A real cell has internal resistance r; the emf ε = V_terminal + Ir, where V_terminal = ε − Ir is the terminal voltage (tending to drop at high current). The efficiency of a cell: η = P_external/P_total = IR/(IR + Ir) = R/(R + r). Maximum power is transferred to the external circuit when R = r (matched load).

Capacitors store charge; capacitance C = Q/V (farads). Energy stored: E = ½CV² = Q²/(2C) = ½QV. In a charging/discharging circuit with resistance R, the charge, voltage, and current vary exponentially with time constant τ = RC: Q = Q₀e^(−t/RC) for discharge; V = V₀(1 − e^(−t/RC)) for charging. At HL, students analyse more complex circuits using Kirchhoff's laws and explore the exponential nature of capacitor charge/discharge quantitatively.

quiz.question: A conductor carries a charge of 90 C in 30 s. Calculate the current. If the resistance is 15 Ω, find the potential difference and the power dissipated.
quiz.markscheme: Current: I = ΔQ/Δt = 90/30 = 3.0 A. Potential difference: V = IR = 3.0 × 15 = 45 V. Power: P = IV = 3.0 × 45 = 135 W. Alternatively: P = I²R = 3.0² × 15 = 9 × 15 = 135 W. Or P = V²/R = 45²/15 = 2025/15 = 135 W. All three formulae are equivalent and interchangeable using Ohm's law.

quiz.question: A 12 V battery is connected to three resistors: 4 Ω and 6 Ω in parallel, combined in series with 2 Ω. Calculate the total resistance, total current, and current through the 6 Ω resistor.
quiz.markscheme: Parallel combination: 1/R_p = 1/4 + 1/6 = 3/12 + 2/12 = 5/12 → R_p = 2.4 Ω. Total resistance: R_T = R_p + 2 = 2.4 + 2 = 4.4 Ω. Total current: I_T = V/R_T = 12/4.4 = 2.73 A. Voltage across parallel combination: V_p = I_T × R_p = 2.73 × 2.4 = 6.55 V. Current through 6 Ω: I₆ = V_p/6 = 6.55/6 = 1.09 A ≈ 1.1 A. Check: I₄ = 6.55/4 = 1.64 A; I₄ + I₆ = 1.64 + 1.09 = 2.73 A ✓ (Kirchhoff's current law).

quiz.question: Apply Kirchhoff's laws to find the current in each branch of a circuit where a 10 V battery (negligible internal resistance) connects two parallel branches: one with 5 Ω and one with 10 Ω.
quiz.markscheme: Both branches have the full 10 V across them (KVL). I₁ = 10/5 = 2.0 A through the 5 Ω. I₂ = 10/10 = 1.0 A through the 10 Ω. Total current from battery (KCL): I_T = I₁ + I₂ = 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.0 A. Total power: P = V × I_T = 10 × 3 = 30 W. Power in 5 Ω: P = I²R = 4 × 5 = 20 W; in 10 Ω: P = 1² × 10 = 10 W. Total = 30 W ✓.

quiz.question: A cell of emf 6.0 V and internal resistance 1.5 Ω is connected to an external resistance of 4.5 Ω. Calculate the terminal voltage and the electrical efficiency of the cell.
quiz.markscheme: Current: I = ε/(R + r) = 6.0/(4.5 + 1.5) = 6.0/6.0 = 1.0 A. Terminal voltage: V = ε − Ir = 6.0 − (1.0)(1.5) = 6.0 − 1.5 = 4.5 V. (Or V = IR = 1.0 × 4.5 = 4.5 V ✓.) Efficiency: η = P_external/P_total = IV/(Iε) = V/ε = 4.5/6.0 = 0.75 = 75%. The other 25% (1.5 W) is dissipated as heat in the internal resistance.

quiz.question: A 100 μF capacitor is charged to 12 V. Calculate the charge stored and the energy stored, and describe what happens to the energy when the capacitor is discharged through a resistor.
quiz.markscheme: Charge: Q = CV = 100 × 10⁻⁶ × 12 = 1.2 × 10⁻³ C = 1.2 mC. Energy: E = ½CV² = ½ × 100 × 10⁻⁶ × 144 = 7.2 × 10⁻³ J = 7.2 mJ. When discharged through a resistor, the electrical energy stored in the capacitor is dissipated as thermal energy (heat) in the resistor. Current flows exponentially: I = I₀e^(−t/RC), so half the stored energy remains after time t = RC ln 2. Eventually all energy is dissipated as heat.

quiz.question: A potential divider consists of a 10 kΩ fixed resistor in series with a thermistor. At 20°C the thermistor has resistance 8 kΩ; at 80°C it has resistance 2 kΩ. The supply voltage is 5.0 V. Calculate V_out across the thermistor at both temperatures and describe how this circuit could be used as a temperature sensor.
quiz.markscheme: At 20°C: V_out = V_in × R_thermistor/(R_fixed + R_thermistor) = 5.0 × 8000/(10 000 + 8000) = 5.0 × 8/18 = 2.22 V. At 80°C: V_out = 5.0 × 2000/(10 000 + 2000) = 5.0 × 2/12 = 0.833 V. As temperature increases, thermistor resistance decreases, so V_out decreases. This voltage can be connected to a comparator or ADC; when V_out falls below a threshold, a circuit could switch on a fan or trigger an alarm, making it a simple over-temperature sensor.

title: Simple harmonic motion
objectives: Define simple harmonic motion (SHM) in terms of restoring force proportional to displacement: F = −kx.
Apply the equations of SHM: x = A cos(ωt + φ), v = −Aω sin(ωt + φ), a = −Aω² cos(ωt + φ).
Derive expressions for the period of a mass–spring system T = 2π√(m/k) and a simple pendulum T = 2π√(L/g).
Describe energy transformations in SHM, showing that E_total = ½kA² = constant at any displacement.
Distinguish between free, damped, and forced oscillations, and describe resonance and its practical implications.
Analyse the phase difference between displacement, velocity, and acceleration in SHM graphs (HL).
summary:
Simple harmonic motion (SHM) occurs when an object experiences a restoring force proportional to and directed opposite to its displacement from equilibrium: F = −kx, where k is a constant (the spring constant for a mass–spring system). The negative sign indicates the force always acts towards equilibrium. This gives Newton's second law as ma = −kx, or a = −(k/m)x = −ω²x, where ω = √(k/m) is the angular frequency in rad s⁻¹.

The general solution is x = A cos(ωt + φ), where A is the amplitude and φ is the initial phase. Velocity v = dx/dt = −Aω sin(ωt + φ) and acceleration a = d²x/dt² = −Aω² cos(ωt + φ) = −ω²x. The period T = 2π/ω and frequency f = ω/(2π). For a mass–spring system: T = 2π√(m/k). For a simple pendulum (small angle approximation, sin θ ≈ θ): T = 2π√(L/g), independent of amplitude and mass.

In SHM, energy is continuously interchanged between kinetic and potential forms. Kinetic energy: E_k = ½mv² = ½mω²(A² − x²). Potential energy: E_p = ½kx² = ½mω²x². Total mechanical energy: E_total = E_k + E_p = ½kA² = ½mω²A² = constant (for undamped SHM). At the equilibrium position (x = 0), E_k is maximum and E_p = 0. At the amplitude (x = ±A), E_k = 0 and E_p = ½kA².

Free oscillations occur at the natural frequency with constant amplitude (no damping). Damped oscillations lose energy to friction or resistance; amplitude decreases over time. Light damping: gradual amplitude decrease, still oscillates. Critical damping: system returns to equilibrium as quickly as possible without oscillating. Overdamping: slow return without oscillation. Forced oscillations occur when a periodic driving force is applied; the system oscillates at the driving frequency. Resonance occurs when the driving frequency equals the natural frequency: amplitude reaches a maximum. Resonance can be useful (e.g. MRI, musical instruments) or destructive (Tacoma Narrows Bridge, vibrating machinery).

quiz.question: State the conditions for simple harmonic motion and write the equation relating acceleration and displacement.
quiz.markscheme: SHM requires: (1) a restoring force that is always directed toward the equilibrium position; (2) the magnitude of the restoring force (and hence the acceleration) is directly proportional to the displacement from equilibrium. The equation is a = −ω²x, where ω is the angular frequency and x is the displacement. The negative sign confirms the acceleration is always directed opposite to the displacement (toward equilibrium). This produces oscillatory motion.

quiz.question: A mass of 0.50 kg is attached to a spring (k = 50 N m⁻¹). Calculate the angular frequency, period, and maximum speed if the amplitude is 0.10 m.
quiz.markscheme: ω = √(k/m) = √(50/0.50) = √100 = 10 rad s⁻¹. Period: T = 2π/ω = 2π/10 = 0.628 s ≈ 0.63 s. Maximum speed (at x = 0): v_max = Aω = 0.10 × 10 = 1.0 m s⁻¹. At maximum displacement v = 0; at equilibrium v = v_max = Aω. Alternatively, using energy: ½mv²_max = ½kA² → v_max = A√(k/m) = 0.10 × 10 = 1.0 m s⁻¹.

quiz.question: A simple pendulum has length 0.25 m. Calculate its period on Earth (g = 9.81 m s⁻²) and on the Moon (g = 1.6 m s⁻²), and explain why the period differs.
quiz.markscheme: Earth: T = 2π√(L/g) = 2π√(0.25/9.81) = 2π√(0.02549) = 2π × 0.1597 = 1.003 s ≈ 1.0 s. Moon: T = 2π√(0.25/1.6) = 2π√(0.15625) = 2π × 0.3953 = 2.485 s ≈ 2.5 s. The period is longer on the Moon because g is weaker — the restoring force (component of gravity) is smaller, so the bob oscillates more slowly. Period is independent of mass and amplitude (small angles only).

quiz.question: Describe the energy transformations in a mass–spring system executing SHM, and calculate the potential energy when the displacement is half the amplitude.
quiz.markscheme: At maximum displacement (x = ±A): all energy is elastic PE = ½kA²; KE = 0. At equilibrium (x = 0): all energy is KE = ½mv²_max = ½kA²; PE = 0. At intermediate positions, both KE and PE contribute. At x = A/2: PE = ½k(A/2)² = ½k × A²/4 = ¼ × ½kA² = ¼ E_total. KE = E_total − PE = ¾ E_total. So at half the amplitude, PE is ¼ of total energy and KE is ¾ of total energy.

quiz.question: Explain resonance and describe one beneficial and one potentially harmful application.
quiz.markscheme: Resonance occurs when the frequency of a periodic driving force matches the natural frequency of the oscillating system. At resonance, the amplitude of oscillation reaches a maximum because energy is most efficiently transferred from the driver to the system. For a lightly damped system, the amplitude at resonance can be very large. Beneficial: MRI scanners use resonance of nuclear spins at their Larmor frequency to produce images. Microwave ovens drive resonance of water molecules. Beneficial in musical instruments — strings and air columns resonate at their natural frequencies. Harmful: the Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940) collapsed due to aerodynamic forcing near its natural frequency. Machinery vibrating at resonant frequencies can cause structural fatigue and failure.

quiz.question: Sketch and describe the phase relationships between displacement, velocity, and acceleration in SHM. (HL)
quiz.markscheme: For x = A cos(ωt): velocity v = −Aω sin(ωt) = Aω cos(ωt + π/2) — velocity leads displacement by π/2 (90°). Acceleration a = −Aω² cos(ωt) = Aω² cos(ωt + π) — acceleration is π (180°) out of phase with displacement (always opposite). On a graph: x and a are in antiphase (opposite); v is π/2 ahead of x. At t = 0 (maximum displacement A), v = 0 and a = −Aω² (maximum, toward equilibrium). At t = T/4 (x = 0), v = −Aω (maximum speed), a = 0.

title: Wave model
objectives: Define wave characteristics: amplitude, wavelength, frequency, period, and wave speed, and apply v = fλ.
Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves, with examples of each.
Describe the principle of superposition and explain constructive and destructive interference.
Define wavefronts and rays, and describe the behaviour of waves at boundaries including reflection and refraction.
Apply Snell's law (n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂) and define the refractive index of a medium.
Explain total internal reflection and calculate the critical angle: sin θ_c = n₂/n₁.
summary:
A wave is a periodic disturbance that transfers energy without transferring matter. Key wave properties: amplitude A (maximum displacement from equilibrium); wavelength λ (distance between adjacent points in phase, e.g. crest to crest); frequency f (oscillations per second, Hz); period T = 1/f; wave speed v = fλ. The speed depends on the medium, not the frequency (for non-dispersive media).

Transverse waves have particle displacement perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. Examples: electromagnetic waves (light, radio), waves on a string. Longitudinal waves have particle displacement parallel to propagation. Example: sound waves in air (compressions and rarefactions). Some waves (surface water waves) have both components.

The principle of superposition: when two or more waves overlap, the resultant displacement at any point is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements. Constructive interference occurs when waves overlap in phase (path difference = nλ, n = 0, 1, 2, ...): amplitude increases. Destructive interference occurs when waves are in antiphase (path difference = (n + ½)λ): amplitude decreases or cancels.

Wavefronts are surfaces of constant phase; rays are lines perpendicular to wavefronts showing wave direction. At a boundary, waves can be reflected (angle of incidence = angle of reflection) or refracted (change in speed causes change in direction). Snell's law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, where n = c/v is the refractive index of a medium (dimensionless; n ≥ 1 for optical media). Light slows and bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium.

Total internal reflection occurs when light attempts to travel from a denser to a less dense medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle θ_c, given by sin θ_c = n₂/n₁ (for n₂ < n₁). At θ > θ_c, all light is reflected. Applications: optical fibres (communications), diamonds (brilliance), endoscopes. Dispersion of light in glass occurs because n depends on wavelength; violet light (shorter λ) has higher n and refracts more than red.

quiz.question: A wave has frequency 440 Hz and wavelength 0.77 m. Calculate its speed and period.
quiz.markscheme: Wave speed: v = fλ = 440 × 0.77 = 338.8 m s⁻¹ ≈ 340 m s⁻¹ (speed of sound in air at ~20°C). Period: T = 1/f = 1/440 = 2.27 × 10⁻³ s ≈ 2.3 ms. The period is the time for one complete oscillation; the frequency is the number of complete oscillations per second. These quantities are reciprocals of each other.

quiz.question: Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves, giving two examples of each.
quiz.markscheme: Transverse waves: particle displacement is perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of energy/wave propagation. Examples: light and other electromagnetic waves; waves on a string or rope; seismic S-waves. Longitudinal waves: particle displacement is parallel (along the same direction) to wave propagation, creating alternating compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure). Examples: sound waves in air; ultrasound; seismic P-waves. EM waves can travel through a vacuum; sound requires a medium.

quiz.question: Two coherent waves arrive at a point with path difference 2.5λ. Determine whether this produces constructive or destructive interference and explain using the principle of superposition.
quiz.markscheme: Path difference = 2.5λ = 2½λ. For constructive interference, path difference = nλ (n integer). For destructive interference, path difference = (n + ½)λ. Since 2.5 = 2 + ½, this is destructive interference: the waves arrive in antiphase (phase difference = π), so the crests of one wave align with the troughs of the other. By the principle of superposition, the displacements cancel: if both waves have the same amplitude A, the resultant amplitude is zero at this point.

quiz.question: A ray of light in air (n = 1.00) strikes a glass surface (n = 1.50) at an angle of incidence of 35°. Calculate the angle of refraction.
quiz.markscheme: Snell's law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂ → sin θ₂ = (n₁/n₂) sin θ₁ = (1.00/1.50) × sin 35° = 0.667 × 0.574 = 0.382. θ₂ = arcsin(0.382) = 22.5° ≈ 23°. The ray bends toward the normal when entering the denser medium (glass, higher n). The angle of refraction (23°) is smaller than the angle of incidence (35°).

quiz.question: Calculate the critical angle for a glass–air interface where n_glass = 1.52, and explain what happens to a ray incident at angles above and below this angle.
quiz.markscheme: sin θ_c = n_air/n_glass = 1.00/1.52 = 0.658 → θ_c = arcsin(0.658) = 41.1° ≈ 41°. For angles of incidence below 41°: the ray is partially refracted into air (and partially reflected). For angles at or above 41°: total internal reflection — all the light is reflected back into the glass, no refracted ray. Applications include optical fibres, which rely on light bouncing along a glass core (higher n) surrounded by cladding (lower n), so light undergoes TIR and travels long distances with minimal loss.

quiz.question: Explain how optical fibres use total internal reflection to transmit data, and identify one advantage and one limitation of this technology.
quiz.markscheme: An optical fibre consists of a glass core (high refractive index) surrounded by cladding (lower refractive index). Light enters the fibre at an angle greater than the critical angle and undergoes repeated total internal reflection along the fibre, with no light escaping. Digital data is encoded as pulses of light and transmitted over long distances with very low loss. Advantage: very high data transmission bandwidth (many wavelengths of light can carry independent signals simultaneously — wavelength division multiplexing); low signal attenuation compared to copper cables. Limitation: fibres can be damaged by bending (reducing TIR); 'modal dispersion' in multimode fibres can cause pulse broadening, limiting data rate over long distances.

title: Wave phenomena
objectives: Describe and explain the diffraction of waves at single slits and apertures, and discuss how wavelength and slit width affect diffraction.
Apply the double-slit interference formula: s = λD/d, where s is fringe spacing, D is screen distance, and d is slit separation.
Explain how a diffraction grating works and apply the grating equation nλ = d sin θ.
Describe polarisation as a property of transverse waves and explain methods of producing polarised light.
Apply Malus's law (I = I₀ cos² θ) to calculate intensity of polarised light passing through an analyser.
summary:
Diffraction is the spreading of waves around obstacles and through apertures. It is most pronounced when the wavelength is comparable to the size of the opening. For a single slit of width b, the first minimum of the diffraction pattern is at sin θ = λ/b. Wider slits produce narrower central maxima; narrower slits produce wider spreading. For circular apertures, the Rayleigh criterion for the limit of resolution is θ_min = 1.22λ/D, where D is the aperture diameter.

Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates wave nature of light through interference. Coherent light of wavelength λ passes through two slits separated by distance d; on a screen at distance D, alternating bright and dark fringes (maxima and minima) are observed. Fringe spacing: s = λD/d. Maxima (constructive) occur where path difference = nλ; minima (destructive) at path difference = (n + ½)λ. This experiment provides a method of measuring the wavelength of light.

A diffraction grating has many parallel slits (typically thousands per mm), producing sharp, widely spaced interference maxima. The grating equation is nλ = d sin θ, where d is the slit spacing (d = 1/N where N is slits per metre), θ is the angle of the nth-order maximum, and n is the order number (n = 0, ±1, ±2, ...). Gratings are used in spectrometers to measure emission and absorption spectra.

Polarisation is a property unique to transverse waves. Unpolarised light has electric field oscillations in all planes perpendicular to the direction of travel. A polariser (e.g. Polaroid filter) transmits only oscillations in one plane — the transmission axis — producing plane-polarised light. Methods of polarisation: transmission through a polaroid filter; reflection at Brewster's angle (tan θ_B = n); scattering (e.g. sky blue is partially polarised). Malus's law: when polarised light of intensity I₀ passes through an analyser with its axis at angle θ to the polarisation direction, the transmitted intensity is I = I₀ cos² θ. At θ = 90°, I = 0 (extinction). Applications include LCD screens, sunglasses, photography filters, and stress analysis in transparent materials.

quiz.question: Explain why a sound wave diffracts around a doorway but visible light does not, using the concept of wavelength compared to aperture size.
quiz.markscheme: Diffraction is significant when wavelength ≈ aperture size. Sound waves have wavelengths of roughly 0.017–17 m (audible range), comparable to a doorway (~0.8–1 m wide). So sound diffracts readily around door frames and corners. Visible light has wavelengths of ~400–700 nm (4–7 × 10⁻⁷ m), far smaller than a doorway, so diffraction is negligible and light appears to travel in straight lines. Diffraction of light is observed only with very narrow slits or very small apertures (< 1 mm).

quiz.question: In a Young's double-slit experiment, slit separation d = 0.50 mm, screen distance D = 1.8 m, and the fringe spacing is 2.2 mm. Calculate the wavelength of light used.
quiz.markscheme: Fringe spacing formula: s = λD/d → λ = sd/D = (2.2 × 10⁻³ × 0.50 × 10⁻³)/1.8 = (1.1 × 10⁻⁶)/1.8 = 6.11 × 10⁻⁷ m = 611 nm ≈ 610 nm. This is in the orange-red region of the visible spectrum. If slit separation were halved (d → 0.25 mm), fringe spacing would double to 4.4 mm, showing the inverse relationship between d and s.

quiz.question: A diffraction grating has 600 lines per mm. Calculate the angle of the second-order maximum for light of wavelength 550 nm.
quiz.markscheme: Slit spacing: d = 1/(600 × 10³) = 1.667 × 10⁻⁶ m. Grating equation: nλ = d sin θ → sin θ = nλ/d = (2 × 550 × 10⁻⁹)/(1.667 × 10⁻⁶) = (1.1 × 10⁻⁶)/(1.667 × 10⁻⁶) = 0.660. θ = arcsin(0.660) = 41.3° ≈ 41°. Check: for n = 3, sin θ = 3 × 0.330 = 0.990, so n = 3 is just possible; n = 4 would require sin θ > 1 (impossible), so n = 3 is the highest order observable.

quiz.question: Describe how plane-polarised light is produced using a polaroid filter and explain Malus's law.
quiz.markscheme: Unpolarised light contains electric field oscillations in all planes perpendicular to the direction of travel. A polaroid filter contains long-chain polymer molecules aligned in one direction, which absorb the component of the electric field parallel to the chains and transmit only the perpendicular component. The transmitted light is plane-polarised, with electric field oscillations confined to one plane (the transmission axis). Malus's law: when this polarised light (intensity I₀) passes through a second polaroid (analyser) whose transmission axis makes angle θ with the polarisation direction, the transmitted intensity is I = I₀ cos² θ. At θ = 0°, I = I₀ (maximum); at θ = 90°, I = 0 (extinction).

quiz.question: Polarised light of intensity 800 W m⁻² is incident on an analyser. Calculate the transmitted intensity when the angle between the polariser and analyser is (a) 30° and (b) 60°.
quiz.markscheme: (a) θ = 30°: I = I₀ cos² θ = 800 × cos² 30° = 800 × (√3/2)² = 800 × 0.75 = 600 W m⁻². (b) θ = 60°: I = 800 × cos² 60° = 800 × (0.5)² = 800 × 0.25 = 200 W m⁻². Note that the intensity at 60° is one-quarter that at 0°, while at 30° it is three-quarters.
