The sum of intensities at complementary angles (30° + 60° = 90°) is I₀: 600 + 200 = 800 ✓ — a consistency check.

title: Standing waves and resonance
objectives: Explain how standing (stationary) waves are formed by the superposition of two identical waves travelling in opposite directions.
Identify nodes and antinodes in standing wave patterns, and describe the motion of particles at each.
Determine the harmonic frequencies of strings fixed at both ends and open and closed pipes.
Apply the relationship between standing wave patterns, wavelength, and wave speed to solve problems.
Explain resonance in the context of standing waves and describe how resonance conditions determine the pitch of musical instruments.
summary:
A standing (stationary) wave is formed when two identical waves travel in opposite directions along the same medium and superpose. Unlike travelling waves, energy is not transported along a standing wave. The pattern consists of nodes (points of permanent zero displacement, where the two waves always cancel) and antinodes (points of maximum amplitude, where the two waves always reinforce). The distance between adjacent nodes (or adjacent antinodes) is λ/2.

For a string fixed at both ends (e.g. guitar string), both ends must be nodes. The allowed wavelengths are λ_n = 2L/n (n = 1, 2, 3, ...) and harmonic frequencies are f_n = nv/(2L) = nf₁, where v is the wave speed on the string and f₁ = v/(2L) is the fundamental frequency. The fundamental (n = 1) has a single antinode at the centre. Harmonics (overtones) are integer multiples of f₁.

For open pipes (both ends open), both ends are antinodes. The resonant frequencies are f_n = nv/(2L), same pattern as strings — all harmonics present. For closed pipes (one end closed, one open), the closed end is a node and the open end is an antinode. The fundamental has wavelength λ₁ = 4L, so f₁ = v/(4L). Only odd harmonics are present: f_n = nv/(4L) where n = 1, 3, 5, .... The closed pipe sounds an octave lower than the open pipe of the same length.

Resonance in musical instruments: pitch is determined by the resonant (natural) frequency of the air column or string. Tightening a string increases wave speed v (v = √(T/μ), where T is tension and μ is linear mass density), raising pitch. Shortening the string (fretting) reduces L, increasing f₁. Changing tube length (trombone, flute keys) adjusts the resonant frequencies of the air column.

quiz.question: Explain how a standing wave is formed on a string, distinguishing nodes from antinodes.
quiz.markscheme: A standing wave forms when two waves of equal amplitude, frequency, and wavelength travel in opposite directions (e.g. an incident wave and its reflection). By the principle of superposition, the two waves add at every point. At nodes, the two waves are always in antiphase and cancel completely — displacement is always zero. At antinodes, the two waves are always in phase and add to give maximum amplitude — displacement oscillates between +2A and −2A. Nodes and antinodes alternate, with spacing λ/4 between a node and adjacent antinode, and λ/2 between successive nodes.

quiz.question: A guitar string of length 0.65 m has a wave speed of 420 m s⁻¹. Calculate the fundamental frequency and the frequency of the third harmonic.
quiz.markscheme: For a string fixed at both ends, fundamental: f₁ = v/(2L) = 420/(2 × 0.65) = 420/1.30 = 323 Hz ≈ 320 Hz. Third harmonic: f₃ = 3f₁ = 3 × 323 = 969 Hz ≈ 970 Hz. In general, f_n = nf₁ for a string, so all integer multiples of the fundamental are present. Higher harmonics (overtones) give the instrument its characteristic timbre (tone colour).

quiz.question: An organ pipe, closed at one end, has length 0.40 m. Taking the speed of sound as 340 m s⁻¹, calculate the fundamental frequency and the next possible resonant frequency.
quiz.markscheme: For a pipe closed at one end: λ₁ = 4L = 4 × 0.40 = 1.60 m. f₁ = v/λ₁ = 340/1.60 = 212.5 Hz ≈ 213 Hz. Only odd harmonics present: next resonant frequency is f₃ = 3f₁ = 3 × 212.5 = 637.5 Hz ≈ 638 Hz (3rd harmonic, λ₃ = 4L/3 = 0.533 m). The even harmonics (f₂, f₄, ...) are absent because no standing wave can form with an even multiple fitting the node-antinode boundary conditions.

quiz.question: Compare the resonant frequencies of an open pipe and a closed pipe of the same length L, with speed of sound v.
quiz.markscheme: Open pipe (both ends antinodes): f_n = nv/(2L) for n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ... — all harmonics present. Fundamental f₁ = v/(2L). Closed pipe (one node, one antinode): f_n = nv/(4L) for n = 1, 3, 5, ... — only odd harmonics. Fundamental f₁ = v/(4L). The closed pipe's fundamental is half that of the open pipe of the same length — it sounds an octave lower. The open pipe has a richer sound because it contains more harmonics. A flute (open at both ends) sounds higher than a clarinet (acts as closed at the mouthpiece end) of similar length.

quiz.question: Explain how changing string tension affects the frequency of a guitar string, using the formula v = √(T/μ).
quiz.markscheme: The wave speed on the string v = √(T/μ), where T is the tension (N) and μ is the linear mass density (kg m⁻¹). The fundamental frequency f₁ = v/(2L) = (1/(2L))√(T/μ). Increasing tension T increases v and therefore increases f₁ — the note rises in pitch. Doubling tension increases v by √2 and raises the frequency by √2 (about 3 semitones). This is how a guitar is tuned: tightening the tuning peg increases tension and raises the pitch, while loosening it lowers the pitch.

title: Doppler effect
objectives: Describe the Doppler effect and explain why it occurs for both sound and light.
Apply the Doppler equation for a moving source: f' = f × v/(v ± v_s), identifying when to use + or −.
Apply the Doppler equation for a moving observer: f' = f × (v ± v_o)/v.
Explain redshift and blueshift in the context of light from astronomical sources.
Apply the approximation Δf/f ≈ v/c for the Doppler shift of electromagnetic waves (HL).
summary:
The Doppler effect is the change in observed frequency of a wave when the source or observer is in motion relative to the medium. When a source moves toward an observer, successive wave fronts are compressed, reducing the effective wavelength and increasing the observed frequency (blueshift for light, higher pitch for sound). When the source moves away, wave fronts are stretched, reducing the observed frequency (redshift, lower pitch).

For sound, with wave speed v in the medium and source moving at v_s: the observed frequency is f' = f × v/(v − v_s) when the source approaches, and f' = f × v/(v + v_s) when the source recedes. For a moving observer (source stationary), moving toward the source: f' = f × (v + v_o)/v; moving away: f' = f × (v − v_o)/v. When both source and observer move, both formulae are combined.

For electromagnetic waves (light), there is no medium, and the Doppler formula must be modified. The exact relativistic Doppler formula is f' = f√[(1 − β)/(1 + β)] for a receding source and f' = f√[(1 + β)/(1 − β)] for an approaching source, where β = v/c. For v ≪ c, the approximation Δf/f ≈ v/c applies, where Δf = f' − f and v is the speed of recession or approach.

Astronomical applications: spectral lines of distant galaxies are shifted toward longer wavelengths (redshift, z = Δλ/λ). The greater the redshift, the faster the galaxy is receding. Hubble's law v = H₀d relates recession speed to distance, showing the universe is expanding. Blueshifted spectra indicate approach (e.g. Andromeda Galaxy approaching the Milky Way). Doppler radar uses reflected microwaves to measure wind speed and vehicle speed; medical ultrasound uses Doppler shifts to measure blood flow velocity.

quiz.question: Explain why a passing ambulance siren changes in pitch, using the Doppler effect.
quiz.markscheme: As the ambulance approaches, it emits sound waves that are compressed in front of it: the wavefronts bunch together, reducing the effective wavelength and increasing the frequency heard by the observer (higher pitch). As the ambulance passes and recedes, wavefronts behind it are stretched, increasing the effective wavelength and decreasing the frequency heard (lower pitch). The siren's actual frequency does not change; only the perceived frequency changes due to the relative motion between source and observer. The effect is more pronounced the faster the source moves relative to the speed of sound.

quiz.question: An ambulance emits a siren at 800 Hz while approaching at 30 m s⁻¹. Calculate the observed frequency. (Speed of sound = 340 m s⁻¹)
quiz.markscheme: Source approaching, observer stationary: f' = f × v/(v − v_s) = 800 × 340/(340 − 30) = 800 × 340/310 = 800 × 1.0968 = 877.4 Hz ≈ 877 Hz. As the ambulance recedes at the same speed: f' = 800 × 340/(340 + 30) = 800 × 340/370 = 800 × 0.9189 = 735 Hz. The pitch drops from 877 Hz to 735 Hz as the source passes — a change of ~142 Hz, clearly audible.

quiz.question: A train whistle emits 500 Hz. An observer moves toward the stationary train at 20 m s⁻¹. Calculate the frequency heard. (Speed of sound = 340 m s⁻¹)
quiz.markscheme: Moving observer, approaching stationary source: f' = f × (v + v_o)/v = 500 × (340 + 20)/340 = 500 × 360/340 = 500 × 1.0588 = 529.4 Hz ≈ 529 Hz. If the observer moved away from the source: f' = 500 × (340 − 20)/340 = 500 × 320/340 = 470.6 Hz ≈ 471 Hz. The pitch is higher when approaching and lower when receding, regardless of whether it's the source or observer that moves.

quiz.question: A galaxy shows a spectral line at 656 nm (Hα, rest wavelength) shifted to 720 nm. Calculate the recession speed and comment on what this implies about the universe.
quiz.markscheme: Redshift: z = Δλ/λ = (720 − 656)/656 = 64/656 = 0.0976 ≈ 0.098. Using Δλ/λ ≈ v/c (valid for v ≪ c): v = zc = 0.0976 × 3.0 × 10⁸ = 2.93 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ ≈ 2.9 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ (about 10% of the speed of light). The galaxy is receding from Earth. Hubble's law (v = H₀d) can then give the distance. Most galaxies show redshift, indicating the universe is expanding uniformly in all directions — evidence for the Big Bang model.

quiz.question: A radar gun emits microwaves at 10.0 GHz. A vehicle reflects the signal with a frequency shift of 1.2 kHz measured. Calculate the speed of the vehicle. (HL) (c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹)
quiz.markscheme: Using the approximation Δf/f ≈ 2v/c (factor of 2 because both the reflection and the source-to-detector path contribute to the Doppler shift in radar): v = cΔf/(2f) = (3.0 × 10⁸ × 1.2 × 10³)/(2 × 10.0 × 10⁹) = (3.6 × 10¹¹)/(2.0 × 10¹⁰) = 18 m s⁻¹ ≈ 18 m s⁻¹ (64.8 km h⁻¹). Alternatively using Δf/f ≈ v/c (single pass): v = cΔf/f = 3.0 × 10⁸ × 1.2 × 10³/(10 × 10⁹) = 36 m s⁻¹. The correct radar formula uses factor of 2 for the round-trip Doppler effect.

title: Gravitational fields
objectives: Define gravitational field strength g as force per unit mass and apply Newton's law of gravitation: F = Gm₁m₂/r².
Calculate gravitational field strength at the surface and above the surface of a spherical mass: g = GM/r².
Define gravitational potential V_grav = −GM/r and relate it to gravitational potential energy E_p = mV = −GMm/r.
Apply conservation of energy to orbital mechanics, including escape speed: v_esc = √(2GM/r).
Derive and apply Kepler's third law: T² ∝ r³, relating orbital period to orbital radius.
Distinguish between gravitational field and potential energy graphs, and describe equipotential surfaces (HL).
summary:
A gravitational field exists in the region of space surrounding any mass, where another mass experiences a gravitational force. Gravitational field strength g at a point is defined as the gravitational force per unit mass placed at that point: g = F/m (N kg⁻¹). Near Earth's surface, g ≈ 9.81 N kg⁻¹. Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻² is the gravitational constant and r is the centre-to-centre distance. For a uniform sphere of mass M, g = GM/r² at distance r from the centre.

Gravitational potential V_grav at a point is the work done per unit mass to bring a small test mass from infinity to that point: V_grav = −GM/r (J kg⁻¹). It is negative because gravity is attractive and work is done by the field (not against it). Gravitational potential energy of a mass m at distance r: E_p = mV = −GMm/r. The negative sign means energy must be supplied to move the mass to a greater distance.

For a circular orbit of radius r, the gravitational force provides the centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r, giving orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and orbital period T = 2πr/v = 2π√(r³/GM). Squaring: T² = (4π²/GM)r³, which is Kepler's third law. Escape speed: the minimum speed needed to escape a gravitational field from the surface: ½mv²_esc = GMm/r → v_esc = √(2GM/r). Note v_esc = √2 × v_orbital.

Gravitational potential energy is negative and increases toward zero as r → ∞. On a V vs r graph, V = −GM/r is a negative curve tending to zero. Equipotential surfaces are spherical shells centred on the mass (in the field of a point mass). No work is done moving along an equipotential. At HL, the field between two masses includes a neutral point where the field strength is zero but the potential has a local maximum (most negative point on a combined potential well).

quiz.question: Calculate the gravitational force between Earth (M = 6.0 × 10²⁴ kg) and the Moon (m = 7.3 × 10²² kg) at a centre-to-centre distance of 3.8 × 10⁸ m. (G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻²)
quiz.markscheme: F = Gm₁m₂/r² = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 6.0 × 10²⁴ × 7.3 × 10²²)/(3.8 × 10⁸)². Numerator: 6.67 × 6.0 × 7.3 × 10⁻¹¹⁺²⁴⁺²² = 292.2 × 10³⁵ = 2.922 × 10³⁷. Denominator: (3.8)² × 10¹⁶ = 14.44 × 10¹⁶ = 1.444 × 10¹⁷. F = 2.922 × 10³⁷/1.444 × 10¹⁷ = 2.02 × 10²⁰ N ≈ 2.0 × 10²⁰ N. This force keeps the Moon in its orbit.

quiz.question: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the surface of Mars (M = 6.4 × 10²³ kg, R = 3.4 × 10⁶ m) and compare it to Earth's g.
quiz.markscheme: g_Mars = GM/R² = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 6.4 × 10²³)/(3.4 × 10⁶)² = (4.27 × 10¹³)/(1.156 × 10¹³) = 3.69 m s⁻² ≈ 3.7 m s⁻². Compared to Earth: g_Mars/g_Earth = 3.7/9.81 ≈ 0.38. The gravitational field on Mars is about 38% that of Earth. This means objects on Mars weigh about 38% of their Earth weight, though their mass is unchanged.

quiz.question: Calculate the orbital period of a satellite in a circular orbit at height 400 km above Earth's surface. (M_E = 6.0 × 10²⁴ kg, R_E = 6.4 × 10⁶ m, G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻²)
quiz.markscheme: Orbital radius: r = R_E + h = 6.4 × 10⁶ + 0.4 × 10⁶ = 6.8 × 10⁶ m. From T² = 4π²r³/(GM): T² = 4π² × (6.8 × 10⁶)³/(6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 6.0 × 10²⁴). Numerator: 4π² × 3.14 × 10²⁰ = 39.48 × 3.14 × 10²⁰ = 1.24 × 10²². Denominator: 4.0 × 10¹⁴. T² = 1.24 × 10²²/4.0 × 10¹⁴ = 3.1 × 10⁷. T = √(3.1 × 10⁷) = 5568 s ≈ 5600 s ≈ 93 min. This is the typical orbital period of the International Space Station.

quiz.question: Calculate the escape speed from the surface of the Moon. (M_Moon = 7.3 × 10²² kg, R_Moon = 1.74 × 10⁶ m)
quiz.markscheme: v_esc = √(2GM/R) = √(2 × 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 7.3 × 10²²/1.74 × 10⁶). Numerator inside root: 2 × 6.67 × 7.3 × 10⁻¹¹⁺²² = 97.4 × 10¹¹ = 9.74 × 10¹². v_esc = √(9.74 × 10¹²/1.74 × 10⁶) = √(5.60 × 10⁶) = 2366 m s⁻¹ ≈ 2400 m s⁻¹ = 2.4 km s⁻¹. This is much less than Earth's escape speed (~11.2 km s⁻¹), explaining why the Moon has no atmosphere (gas molecules can easily reach escape speed at lunar temperatures).

quiz.question: State Kepler's third law and verify it for Earth (T = 365 days, r = 1.5 × 10¹¹ m) and Mars (T = 687 days, r = 2.28 × 10¹¹ m).
quiz.markscheme: Kepler's third law: T² ∝ r³, or T²/r³ = constant = 4π²/(GM_Sun). For Earth: T²/r³ = (365)²/(1.5 × 10¹¹)³. In SI: T_E = 3.156 × 10⁷ s; r_E³ = 3.375 × 10³³ m³; ratio = (3.156 × 10⁷)²/(3.375 × 10³³) = 9.96 × 10¹⁴/3.375 × 10³³ = 2.95 × 10⁻¹⁹. For Mars: T_M = 5.94 × 10⁷ s; r_M³ = (2.28 × 10¹¹)³ = 1.187 × 10³⁴; ratio = (5.94 × 10⁷)²/1.187 × 10³⁴ = 3.53 × 10¹⁵/1.187 × 10³⁴ = 2.97 × 10⁻¹⁹. Both ratios ≈ 2.96 × 10⁻¹⁹, confirming Kepler's third law.

quiz.question: Describe the shape of a gravitational potential (V vs r) graph for a point mass and explain the significance of equipotential surfaces. (HL)
quiz.markscheme: The gravitational potential V = −GM/r is negative for all finite r and tends to zero as r → ∞. On a V vs r graph, the curve starts at a large negative value near the surface and asymptotically approaches zero. Equipotential surfaces are spherical shells centred on the mass; all points on the surface have the same V. No work is done moving along an equipotential (F · ds = 0). The gravitational field g = −dV/dr is the negative gradient of the potential; where equipotentials are closely spaced (near the mass), g is large. For the combined field of two masses, there is a saddle point (neutral point) between them where g = 0 but V has a maximum negative value.

title: Electric and magnetic fields
objectives: Define electric field strength E = F/q and apply Coulomb's law F = kq₁q₂/r² to point charges.
Calculate electric field and potential for point charges, and describe uniform fields between parallel plates.
Define magnetic flux density B and apply the force on a current-carrying conductor F = BIL sin θ and on a charged particle F = qvB sin θ.
Describe the patterns of electric and magnetic field lines for common configurations.
Apply the relationship between electric field and potential: E = −ΔV/Δx.
Explain how a mass spectrometer, velocity selector, and cyclotron use electric and magnetic fields.
summary:
An electric field is a region where a charged particle experiences a force. Electric field strength E = F/q (N C⁻¹ = V m⁻¹), defined as the force per unit positive charge. For a point charge Q, Coulomb's law gives the force on charge q at distance r: F = kq₁q₂/r² where k = 1/(4πε₀) = 8.99 × 10⁹ N m² C⁻². The field from a point charge: E = kQ/r². Field lines run from positive to negative charges; uniform fields exist between parallel plates.

Electric potential V at a point is the work done per unit positive charge moving from infinity to that point: V = kQ/r (volts). The potential difference ΔV between two points is the energy per unit charge transferred. Electric field and potential are related: E = −ΔV/Δx (the field is the negative gradient of potential). Equipotential lines are perpendicular to field lines.

A magnetic field exerts a force on a current-carrying conductor: F = BIL sin θ, where B is magnetic flux density (tesla, T), I is current, L is the conductor length in the field, and θ is the angle between the current direction and the field. Fleming's left-hand rule gives the direction of the force. For a particle of charge q moving at speed v in a magnetic field: F = qvB sin θ. The magnetic force is always perpendicular to v, so it does no work and only changes direction — producing circular motion: r = mv/(qB).

A velocity selector uses crossed electric and magnetic fields: E force (qE) and B force (qvB) balance for selected speed v = E/B. A mass spectrometer separates ions by mass/charge ratio using this principle followed by circular motion in B: m/q = Br/v. A cyclotron accelerates particles in a spiral using alternating electric fields between D-shaped regions (dees), with a perpendicular magnetic field providing circular motion; radius increases as speed increases. Electric field lines from a positive point charge radiate outward; magnetic field lines around a long wire are concentric circles (right-hand screw rule). The Earth's magnetic field protects the atmosphere from solar wind by deflecting charged particles.

quiz.question: Calculate the electric force between two charges q₁ = +3.0 μC and q₂ = −2.0 μC separated by 0.15 m in air. (k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N m² C⁻²)
quiz.markscheme: F = kq₁q₂/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 3.0 × 10⁻⁶ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶)/(0.15)². Numerator: 8.99 × 10⁹ × 6.0 × 10⁻¹² = 8.99 × 6.0 × 10⁻³ = 53.94 × 10⁻³ = 0.05394. Denominator: 0.0225. F = 0.05394/0.0225 = 2.40 N. The force is attractive (opposite charges) with magnitude 2.40 N directed along the line joining the charges. By Newton's third law, q₂ also exerts a 2.40 N force on q₁.

quiz.question: Parallel plates are separated by 5.0 mm and have a potential difference of 200 V. Calculate the uniform electric field strength between the plates and the force on a proton placed between them.
quiz.markscheme: E = ΔV/Δx = 200/(5.0 × 10⁻³) = 40 000 V m⁻¹ = 4.0 × 10⁴ N C⁻¹. Force on proton: F = qE = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁴ = 6.4 × 10⁻¹⁵ N, directed from the positive plate toward the negative plate (in the direction of the field for a positive charge). This field is uniform between the plates (field lines are parallel and equally spaced).

quiz.question: A wire of length 0.30 m carrying 5.0 A is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.40 T. Calculate the force on the wire.
quiz.markscheme: F = BIL sin θ = 0.40 × 5.0 × 0.30 × sin 90° = 0.40 × 5.0 × 0.30 × 1 = 0.60 N. The force is perpendicular to both the current direction and the field direction, determined by Fleming's left-hand rule: first finger = field direction, second finger = current direction, thumb = force (thrust) direction. If the wire is not perpendicular (θ ≠ 90°), the force is reduced by factor sin θ.

quiz.question: A proton (q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, m = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg) moves at 2.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.50 T. Calculate the radius of its circular path.
quiz.markscheme: The magnetic force provides centripetal force: qvB = mv²/r → r = mv/(qB). r = (1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ × 2.0 × 10⁶)/(1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 0.50) = (3.34 × 10⁻²¹)/(8.0 × 10⁻²⁰) = 0.04175 m ≈ 0.042 m = 4.2 cm. Note: the magnetic force does no work on the proton (F ⊥ v), so the speed remains constant and the path is a perfect circle of constant radius.

quiz.question: Describe the operation of a velocity selector and explain how it can be used to determine the charge-to-mass ratio of an ion.
quiz.markscheme: A velocity selector has a uniform electric field E (from parallel plates) and a uniform magnetic field B (perpendicular to both E and the particle's velocity). For a particle moving through the selector: electric force qE acts one way, magnetic force qvB acts the other. They balance when qE = qvB → v = E/B — only particles with speed v = E/B pass straight through; others are deflected. After selection, the beam enters a region with only field B. The particle undergoes circular motion: r = mv/(qB) → m/q = Br/v = B²r/E. By measuring r (radius of circular path), knowing B and E, the charge-to-mass ratio can be determined. This is the principle of the mass spectrometer.

quiz.question: Sketch and compare the electric field line patterns for (a) a positive point charge, (b) two equal positive charges, and (c) a positive and a negative charge of equal magnitude.
quiz.markscheme: (a) Positive point charge: field lines radiate outward in all directions (spherically symmetric for a 3D diagram; radially outward in 2D cross-section). Lines are closer together near the charge (stronger field). (b) Two equal positive charges: lines repel between the charges; there is a neutral point midway between them where field strength = 0. Lines curve away from each charge. (c) Positive and negative charges (electric dipole): lines leave the positive charge and enter the negative charge; lines are most dense between the charges (strongest field); the pattern is like the D-shape of a bar magnet field, suggesting attraction. In all cases, field lines are perpendicular to equipotential surfaces and never cross each other.

title: Motion in electromagnetic fields
objectives: Analyse the motion of charged particles in uniform electric fields, including deflection of electrons in a cathode ray tube.
Describe the Hall effect and apply the formula V_H = IB/(nqt) to determine carrier concentration.
Explain the principle of electromagnetic induction and apply Faraday's law: ε = −ΔΦ/Δt.
Apply Lenz's law to determine the direction of an induced current or force.
Describe the operation of a simple AC generator and a transformer, applying V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s.
summary:
A charged particle entering a uniform electric field perpendicular to the field experiences constant acceleration parallel to the field (F = qE) while its perpendicular velocity remains unchanged — the motion is parabolic, analogous to projectile motion. A cathode ray tube (CRT) exploits this: electrons accelerated through potential V acquire speed v = √(2eV/m), then are deflected by electric (or magnetic) fields to hit a phosphor screen.

The Hall effect occurs when current flows through a conductor in a perpendicular magnetic field. The magnetic force on charge carriers (F = qvB) causes them to accumulate on one side, creating a transverse potential difference (Hall voltage): V_H = IB/(nqt), where n is the number density of charge carriers, q is carrier charge, and t is the thickness of the conductor. The polarity of V_H identifies the sign of the majority charge carrier. The Hall probe is used to measure magnetic field strength.

Electromagnetic induction (Faraday): when the magnetic flux Φ = BA cos θ through a circuit changes, an emf is induced: ε = −N dΦ/dt (Faraday's law), where N is the number of turns. The negative sign (Lenz's law) means the induced current opposes the change causing it (conservation of energy). Lenz's law: the induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the change in flux. This manifests as a braking force on a moving conductor.

An AC generator uses electromagnetic induction: a coil rotating in a magnetic field produces a sinusoidal emf ε = NBAω sin(ωt) = ε₀ sin(ωt), where ε₀ = NBAω is the peak emf. A transformer changes AC voltage: V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s (voltage ratio equals turns ratio). For an ideal transformer, power is conserved: V_p I_p = V_s I_s. Step-up transformers increase voltage (and decrease current) for efficient long-distance power transmission; step-down transformers reduce voltage for domestic use. Power losses in transmission lines: P_loss = I²R; transmitting at high voltage (low current) minimizes resistive losses.

quiz.question: An electron is accelerated through 500 V and then enters a uniform electric field of 3.0 × 10⁴ V m⁻¹ perpendicular to its motion. Calculate its entry speed and describe its subsequent path. (e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, m_e = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)
quiz.markscheme: Entry speed: eV = ½mv² → v = √(2eV/m) = √(2 × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500/9.11 × 10⁻³¹) = √(1.6 × 10⁻¹⁶/9.11 × 10⁻³¹) = √(1.756 × 10¹⁴) = 1.325 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ ≈ 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. In the deflecting field, the electron experiences constant force F = eE = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 3.0 × 10⁴ = 4.8 × 10⁻¹⁵ N perpendicular to motion, giving acceleration a = F/m_e = 5.27 × 10¹⁵ m s⁻². The path is parabolic, curving toward the positive plate (electron is negatively charged, attracted to positive plate).

quiz.question: Explain the Hall effect and state how the Hall voltage can be used to determine the sign of charge carriers in a semiconductor.
quiz.markscheme: When conventional current I flows through a conductor of thickness t in a perpendicular magnetic field B, the magnetic force F = qvB acts on the charge carriers, pushing them to one side of the conductor. This creates an uneven charge distribution: a transverse electric field builds up (Hall field) until it balances the magnetic force: qE_H = qvB → E_H = vB. The Hall voltage V_H = E_H × t = vBt. Since current I = nqvA (A = cross-section), v = I/(nqA). So V_H = IB/(nqt). If the top face becomes positive, carriers are positive (conventional current direction); if the top face becomes negative, carriers are electrons (negative charge moving opposite to conventional current direction). This distinguishes p-type (hole) from n-type (electron) semiconductors.

quiz.question: A rectangular coil of 50 turns and area 0.020 m² is placed in a magnetic field of 0.40 T. The coil is pulled out of the field in 0.25 s. Calculate the average induced emf.
quiz.markscheme: Initial flux: Φ₁ = BA = 0.40 × 0.020 = 8.0 × 10⁻³ Wb. Final flux: Φ₂ = 0 (coil completely removed). Change in flux: ΔΦ = 0 − 8.0 × 10⁻³ = −8.0 × 10⁻³ Wb. Faraday's law: |ε| = N|ΔΦ/Δt| = 50 × 8.0 × 10⁻³/0.25 = 50 × 0.032 = 1.6 V. By Lenz's law, the induced current in the coil will flow in the direction that creates a field to maintain the original flux (opposing its decrease), resulting in a braking force on the coil opposing its removal.

quiz.question: Apply Lenz's law to explain why a copper ring falls more slowly when dropped over a bar magnet than when dropped freely, and calculate the braking force if the induced emf is 0.020 V and the ring has resistance 0.05 Ω.
quiz.markscheme: As the ring falls toward the magnet, the flux through the ring increases. By Lenz's law, the induced current opposes this increase — it flows in a direction to create a magnetic field opposing the magnet's field (repelling the ring). This creates an upward force opposing the motion. Induced current I = ε/R = 0.020/0.05 = 0.40 A. The braking force = BIL (force on the current-carrying ring in the field). The ring falls at terminal velocity when this upward magnetic braking force equals the weight of the ring (net force = 0), analogous to terminal velocity in fluid resistance — demonstrating conservation of energy: the gravitational PE lost equals the electrical energy dissipated as heat in the ring.

quiz.question: A transformer has 500 primary turns and 50 secondary turns. The primary is connected to 230 V AC. Calculate the secondary voltage and the secondary current if the primary current is 0.10 A (assume 100% efficiency).
quiz.markscheme: Turns ratio: N_p/N_s = 500/50 = 10. V_s = V_p × (N_s/N_p) = 230 × (50/500) = 230 × 0.1 = 23 V. This is a step-down transformer (voltage reduced by factor 10). For 100% efficiency, input power = output power: V_p I_p = V_s I_s → I_s = V_p I_p/V_s = 230 × 0.10/23 = 1.0 A. Current increases by factor 10 as voltage decreases by factor 10, consistent with power conservation: P = 230 × 0.10 = 23 W = 23 × 1.0 = 23 W ✓.

title: Induction
objectives: Define magnetic flux Φ = BA cos θ and apply Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction: ε = −N dΦ/dt.
Apply Lenz's law and explain it as a consequence of conservation of energy.
Describe mutual inductance and self-inductance, and apply ε = −L dI/dt for a coil.
Analyse the behaviour of inductors in DC circuits, including the time constant τ = L/R for LR circuits.
Explain the production and transmission of AC power, including transformers and their role in power grids.
Apply the concepts of peak and rms values of AC current and voltage: V_rms = V₀/√2, I_rms = I₀/√2.
summary:
Magnetic flux through a surface is Φ = BA cos θ (Weber, Wb = T m²), where B is the flux density, A is the area, and θ is the angle between B and the normal to the surface. Faraday's law: the induced emf in a circuit is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage ε = −N dΦ/dt. The magnitude of the emf is |ε| = N|ΔΦ/Δt| for a coil of N turns. Lenz's law provides the direction of the induced emf: it is such that the induced current opposes the change in flux that produces it (conservation of energy — the source must do work against the induced braking force).

Self-inductance L (henry, H) measures a coil's ability to induce an emf in itself due to its own changing current: ε = −L dI/dt. The energy stored in an inductor carrying current I: E = ½LI². Mutual inductance M between two coils: ε₂ = −M dI₁/dt. In an LR series circuit, when a DC source is switched on, the current builds up exponentially with time constant τ = L/R: I(t) = I₀(1 − e^(−t/τ)), where I₀ = V/R. When the source is disconnected, current decays as I(t) = I₀ e^(−t/τ).

AC generators produce a sinusoidal output: ε(t) = ε₀ sin(ωt), where ε₀ = NBAω is the peak emf. The rms (root mean square) value is the DC equivalent that delivers the same power: V_rms = V₀/√2 ≈ 0.707 V₀; I_rms = I₀/√2. Power in AC circuit: P = V_rms I_rms cos φ (where φ is the phase angle between V and I; for resistive loads cos φ = 1).

Transformers operate on mutual induction between primary and secondary coils. For an ideal transformer: V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s = I_s/I_p. Transformers enable electricity to be transmitted at high voltage (low current) through the national grid, minimising resistive losses (P_loss = I²R). Step-up transformers at power stations increase voltage to ~400 kV; step-down transformers at substations reduce it for domestic use (~230 V). This is one key advantage of AC over DC for power distribution.

quiz.question: Calculate the magnetic flux through a rectangular loop of area 0.060 m² placed at 40° to a magnetic field of 0.80 T.
quiz.markscheme: Φ = BA cos θ. The angle θ is between the field B and the normal to the plane of the loop. If the loop plane is at 40° to B, the normal is at 90° − 40° = 50° to B. Φ = 0.80 × 0.060 × cos 50° = 0.048 × 0.643 = 0.0309 Wb ≈ 0.031 Wb. Note: if θ is given as the angle between the field and the plane (not the normal), use Φ = BA sin θ = 0.80 × 0.060 × sin 40° = 0.048 × 0.643 = 0.031 Wb (same numerical result in this case).

quiz.question: Explain Lenz's law as a consequence of conservation of energy, using the example of a magnet being pushed into a coil.
quiz.markscheme: When a magnet is pushed toward a coil, the increasing flux induces an emf. By Lenz's law, the induced current flows in a direction to create a magnetic field opposing the magnet's approach — the near face of the coil acts as a like pole, repelling the magnet. To push the magnet into the coil, work must be done against this repulsive force. This work equals the electrical energy dissipated in the coil's resistance. If the induced current instead aided the magnet's motion (Lenz's law violated), the magnet would accelerate indefinitely, creating energy from nothing — violating conservation of energy. Lenz's law is therefore a direct consequence of the energy principle.

quiz.question: An inductor of L = 50 mH is in series with a 25 Ω resistor, connected to a 12 V battery. Calculate the time constant, final steady-state current, and the current 4 ms after switching on.
quiz.markscheme: Time constant: τ = L/R = 50 × 10⁻³/25 = 2.0 × 10⁻³ s = 2.0 ms. Final current: I₀ = V/R = 12/25 = 0.48 A. Current at t = 4 ms: I(t) = I₀(1 − e^(−t/τ)) = 0.48 × (1 − e^(−4/2)) = 0.48 × (1 − e^(−2)) = 0.48 × (1 − 0.135) = 0.48 × 0.865 = 0.415 A ≈ 0.42 A. After one time constant (t = 2 ms): I = 0.48 × (1 − e⁻¹) = 0.48 × 0.632 = 0.303 A (63.2% of final value — consistent with exponential approach).

quiz.question: The UK mains supply has a peak voltage of 325 V. Calculate the rms voltage, rms current through a 1200 W appliance, and its resistance.
quiz.markscheme: V_rms = V₀/√2 = 325/√2 = 325/1.414 = 229.9 V ≈ 230 V. Power P = V_rms × I_rms → I_rms = P/V_rms = 1200/230 = 5.22 A ≈ 5.2 A. Resistance: R = V_rms/I_rms = 230/5.22 = 44.1 Ω ≈ 44 Ω. Alternatively: R = V_rms²/P = 230²/1200 = 52900/1200 = 44.1 Ω. The rms values are the equivalent DC values for power calculations; the peak current is I₀ = I_rms × √2 = 5.22 × 1.414 = 7.38 A.

quiz.question: Explain why electricity is transmitted at high voltage in the national grid and calculate the power loss in a transmission cable (R = 20 Ω) when transmitting 1.0 MW at (a) 500 V and (b) 50 000 V.
quiz.markscheme: High voltage reduces current for the same power (P = VI): lower current means less resistive power loss (P_loss = I²R). (a) At 500 V: I = P/V = 1.0 × 10⁶/500 = 2000 A. P_loss = I²R = (2000)² × 20 = 4 × 10⁶ × 20 = 80 MW — far more than the power being transmitted! (b) At 50 000 V: I = 1.0 × 10⁶/50 000 = 20 A. P_loss = (20)² × 20 = 400 × 20 = 8000 W = 8 kW — only 0.8% of the transmitted power. This illustrates why transformers (step-up before transmission, step-down before distribution) are essential in AC power grids.

quiz.question: Describe the energy stored in an inductor and calculate the energy stored in an inductor L = 0.20 H carrying a current of 3.0 A. Explain what happens to this energy when the circuit is suddenly broken.
quiz.markscheme: Energy: E = ½LI² = ½ × 0.20 × (3.0)² = ½ × 0.20 × 9 = 0.90 J. When the circuit is suddenly broken, the current cannot instantaneously fall to zero (the inductor opposes changes in current). The inductor attempts to maintain the current, inducing a large emf (ε = −L dI/dt; with dI/dt very large, ε is very large). In practice, this large voltage causes a spark across the switch contacts. The stored magnetic energy is rapidly dissipated as heat and light in the spark, or in a protective diode placed across the inductor to provide a current path.

title: Structure of the atom
objectives: Describe the Rutherford scattering experiment and explain how it provided evidence for the nuclear model of the atom.
State the composition of the nucleus (protons and neutrons) and define atomic number Z, mass number A, and neutron number N.
Explain atomic line spectra in terms of electron transitions between discrete energy levels: ΔE = hf.
Apply the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom: energy levels E_n = −13.6/n² eV.
Describe nuclear reactions using conservation of charge number and mass number.
Explain the existence of nuclear binding energy and apply E = mc² to mass–energy equivalence.
summary:
In 1909, Geiger, Marsden, and Rutherford fired alpha particles at thin gold foil. Most passed straight through; some deflected at large angles; a very few bounced back. Rutherford concluded that the atom is mostly empty space, with a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus containing almost all the mass. This replaced the Thomson 'plum pudding' model with the nuclear model.

The nucleus contains Z protons and N neutrons; mass number A = Z + N. Atoms of the same element with different N are isotopes. Nuclear size is ~10⁻¹⁵ m (femtometre, fm); atomic radius ~10⁻¹⁰ m (ångström). Nuclear density is approximately constant at ~2.3 × 10¹⁷ kg m⁻³ because nuclear volume ∝ A and mass ∝ A.

Electrons occupy discrete energy levels around the nucleus. In the Bohr model for hydrogen: E_n = −13.6/n² eV, where n = 1, 2, 3, ... is the principal quantum number. The ground state is n = 1 (E = −13.6 eV); the ionisation energy is 13.6 eV. When an electron transitions from level n₂ to n₁ (n₂ > n₁ > emission), a photon is emitted with frequency f given by: ΔE = E_upper − E_lower = hf = hc/λ. Absorption spectra arise from atoms absorbing photons and exciting electrons to higher levels. Emission spectra (line spectra) are the reverse. Each element has a unique spectral fingerprint.

Nuclear binding energy is the energy needed to completely separate all nucleons. It equals the mass defect × c²: E_binding = Δm × c², where Δm = (Zm_p + Nm_n) − m_nucleus is the mass defect. The binding energy per nucleon peaks at around iron-56, explaining why both fission and fusion can release energy. Nuclear reactions conserve charge number (total Z) and mass number (total A). At HL, nuclear energy levels and gamma emission extend the discussion of discrete energy quantisation.

quiz.question: Describe the Rutherford alpha-scattering experiment and explain what each observation tells us about atomic structure.
quiz.markscheme: Setup: a source of alpha particles aimed at a thin gold foil; detectors placed at various angles. Observations and inferences: (1) Most alphas pass straight through → the atom is mostly empty space. (2) A small fraction are deflected at large angles → there is a concentrated region of positive charge (the nucleus) that repels the alpha particles. (3) A very small fraction (about 1 in 20 000) scatter back at near 180° → the nucleus is much more massive than the alpha particle (it barely recoils) and is very small (the nucleus occupies about 10⁻¹⁵/10⁻¹⁰ = 10⁻⁵ of the atomic diameter). This led to the nuclear model: a tiny, massive, positively charged nucleus surrounded by distant, orbiting electrons.

quiz.question: An electron in a hydrogen atom falls from the n = 4 level to the n = 2 level. Calculate the wavelength of the emitted photon. (E_n = −13.6/n² eV, h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J)
quiz.markscheme: E₄ = −13.6/16 = −0.850 eV. E₂ = −13.6/4 = −3.40 eV. ΔE = E₄ − E₂ = −0.850 − (−3.40) = 2.55 eV = 2.55 × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 4.08 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Frequency: f = ΔE/h = 4.08 × 10⁻¹⁹/6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ = 6.15 × 10¹⁴ Hz. Wavelength: λ = c/f = 3.0 × 10⁸/6.15 × 10¹⁴ = 4.88 × 10⁻⁷ m = 488 nm (blue-green visible light — part of the Balmer series).

quiz.question: Calculate the binding energy of helium-4 nucleus. (m_p = 1.6726 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, m_n = 1.6749 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, m_He-4 = 6.6447 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹)
quiz.markscheme: He-4 has Z = 2 protons and N = 2 neutrons. Mass of free nucleons: 2m_p + 2m_n = 2(1.6726) + 2(1.6749) = 3.3452 + 3.3498 = 6.6950 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. Mass defect: Δm = 6.6950 − 6.6447 = 0.0503 × 10⁻²⁷ kg = 5.03 × 10⁻²⁹ kg. Binding energy: E = Δmc² = 5.03 × 10⁻²⁹ × (3.0 × 10⁸)² = 5.03 × 10⁻²⁹ × 9.0 × 10¹⁶ = 4.527 × 10⁻¹² J ≈ 4.5 × 10⁻¹² J ≈ 28.3 MeV. Binding energy per nucleon = 28.3/4 ≈ 7.1 MeV/nucleon.

quiz.question: Write a balanced nuclear equation for the alpha decay of radium-226 (Z = 88) and identify the daughter nucleus.
quiz.markscheme: Alpha particle: ⁴₂He (or ⁴₂α), Z = 2, A = 4. Conservation of mass number: 226 = 4 + A_daughter → A_daughter = 222. Conservation of charge number: 88 = 2 + Z_daughter → Z_daughter = 86. Element with Z = 86 is radon (Rn). Equation: ²²⁶₈₈Ra → ²²²₈₆Rn + ⁴₂He. The daughter nucleus is radon-222. Alpha decay occurs when the nucleus is too large and nucleon binding energy can be increased by emitting a helium-4 nucleus.

quiz.question: Explain the concept of binding energy per nucleon and use it to explain why both fission and fusion can release energy.
quiz.markscheme: Binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) is the energy required to remove one nucleon from the nucleus, on average. A higher BE/A means a more stable nucleus. The BE/A curve peaks at approximately 8.8 MeV/nucleon for iron-56 (A ≈ 56). For light nuclei (low A), BE/A increases with A — fusing two light nuclei (e.g. deuterium + tritium → helium-4) produces a product with higher BE/A; the extra binding energy is released as kinetic energy. For heavy nuclei (high A, e.g. uranium-235), BE/A decreases — splitting the nucleus (fission) produces fragments with higher BE/A; the energy difference is released. In both cases, the products are more tightly bound (lower rest mass) and the mass defect × c² is released as energy.

quiz.question: Describe how the line emission spectrum of hydrogen provides evidence for discrete energy levels in the atom.
quiz.markscheme: When hydrogen gas is excited (e.g. by electrical discharge), it emits light only at specific wavelengths — a series of discrete spectral lines rather than a continuous spectrum. This can only be explained if electrons occupy discrete energy levels and not a continuous range of energies. Each spectral line corresponds to an electron transition between two specific energy levels; ΔE = hf, so each transition emits a photon of a single specific frequency (and wavelength). The Balmer series (visible, n → 2) gives lines at 656 nm (red), 486 nm (blue-green), 434 nm (violet), etc. The exact wavelengths agree precisely with the Bohr model predictions: E_n = −13.6/n² eV. No classical model (continuous orbits) can explain this discrete spectrum.

title: Quantum physics
objectives: Describe the photoelectric effect and explain why it cannot be explained by classical wave theory.
Apply the photoelectric equation: E_k(max) = hf − φ, where φ is the work function.
Describe wave–particle duality and apply the de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/p = h/(mv).
Explain the Heisenberg uncertainty principle: Δx Δp ≥ ħ/2 and ΔE Δt ≥ ħ/2.
Describe the double-slit experiment with electrons and interpret the probability interpretation of the wavefunction.
Outline the tunnel effect and describe its applications in scanning tunnelling microscopy and alpha decay.
summary:
The photoelectric effect: light incident on a metal surface ejects electrons (photoelectrons). Classical wave theory predicts that any frequency of light (given sufficient intensity) should eventually eject electrons, and that the kinetic energy of ejected electrons should increase with intensity. Experimentally: no photoelectrons are emitted below a threshold frequency f₀, regardless of intensity; kinetic energy depends on frequency, not intensity; emission is instantaneous. Einstein (1905) explained this by treating light as photons, each of energy E = hf. A photon ejects one electron; the minimum energy to escape the surface is the work function φ. Maximum kinetic energy of ejected electrons: E_k(max) = hf − φ. Threshold: hf₀ = φ. Stopping potential V_s: eV_s = E_k(max).

Wave–particle duality: light exhibits both wave properties (interference, diffraction) and particle properties (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering). Matter also has wave properties; the de Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of momentum p = mv is λ = h/p. Electron diffraction confirms this: electrons directed at a crystal lattice (spacing ~10⁻¹⁰ m) produce diffraction patterns, only explicable if λ = h/(mv) is comparable to the lattice spacing.

Heisenberg's uncertainty principle places a fundamental limit on simultaneously knowing position and momentum: Δx Δp ≥ ħ/2 (where ħ = h/(2π)). A more precise position measurement (smaller Δx) necessarily disturbs momentum more (larger Δp). This is not a measurement limitation but a fundamental property of quantum systems. Similarly, ΔE Δt ≥ ħ/2 — energy and time cannot both be precisely determined simultaneously.

In the double-slit experiment with electrons (one at a time), an interference pattern builds up on a detector screen after many electrons — each electron goes through both slits simultaneously as a wavefunction and interferes with itself. The wavefunction |ψ|² gives the probability density for finding the particle at a given location. This probability interpretation (Born interpretation) means only the probability of outcomes can be predicted, not individual events.

Quantum tunnelling: a particle can penetrate and pass through a potential energy barrier even when its kinetic energy is less than the barrier height — classically forbidden but quantum-mechanically allowed because the wavefunction has a non-zero amplitude inside and beyond the barrier. Applications: scanning tunnelling microscopy (STM) — tunnelling current between tip and surface maps atomic topography; alpha decay — alpha particles tunnel through the Coulomb barrier of the nucleus despite lacking sufficient classical energy to escape.

quiz.question: Describe three features of the photoelectric effect that classical wave theory cannot explain, and explain how the photon model accounts for each.
quiz.markscheme: (1) Threshold frequency: classical theory predicts any frequency (given enough intensity) should eject electrons. Photon model: only photons with E = hf ≥ φ (work function) have enough energy to eject one electron; below f₀ = φ/h, no single photon has sufficient energy. (2) Kinetic energy independent of intensity: classical theory predicts higher intensity should give electrons more energy. Photon model: intensity determines the number of photons per second (more electrons ejected), not each photon's energy; E_k = hf − φ depends only on frequency. (3) Instantaneous emission: classical theory predicts a time delay (energy must accumulate). Photon model: a single photon immediately transfers all its energy to one electron — no accumulation needed.

quiz.question: Light of wavelength 250 nm hits a metal surface with work function 3.5 eV. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons and the stopping potential. (h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹)
Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through 100 V. (m_e = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s)
quiz.markscheme: Energy gained: E_k = eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 100 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁷ J. Speed: v = √(2E_k/m) = √(2 × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁷/9.11 × 10⁻³¹) = √(3.513 × 10¹³) = 5.927 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹. Momentum: p = mv = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 5.927 × 10⁶ = 5.400 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹. de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴/5.400 × 10⁻²⁴ = 1.228 × 10⁻¹⁰ m ≈ 0.12 nm. This is comparable to atomic spacings in crystals (~0.2 nm), explaining why electrons produce diffraction patterns with crystal targets.

quiz.question: State the Heisenberg uncertainty principle and apply it to estimate the minimum kinetic energy of an electron confined to a nucleus of diameter 10 fm.
quiz.markscheme: Uncertainty principle: Δx Δp ≥ ħ/2 (ħ = h/(2π) = 1.055 × 10⁻³⁴ J s). If electron confined to Δx = 10 × 10⁻¹⁵ m = 10⁻¹⁴ m: Δp ≥ ħ/(2Δx) = 1.055 × 10⁻³⁴/(2 × 10⁻¹⁴) = 5.28 × 10⁻²¹ kg m s⁻¹. Minimum kinetic energy: E_k = (Δp)²/(2m_e) = (5.28 × 10⁻²¹)²/(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹) = 2.79 × 10⁻⁴¹/1.82 × 10⁻³⁰ = 1.53 × 10⁻¹¹ J ≈ 95 MeV. This huge energy far exceeds what nuclear forces could provide, showing electrons cannot exist in the nucleus — validating the neutron model over the proton-electron nucleus model.

quiz.question: Describe the quantum tunnelling effect and explain how it enables alpha decay despite the alpha particle lacking sufficient energy to escape the nucleus classically.
quiz.markscheme: Quantum tunnelling: a quantum particle (described by a wavefunction) can penetrate a potential energy barrier even when its total energy is less than the barrier height. The wavefunction does not drop to zero at the barrier; it decays exponentially inside but has a non-zero amplitude on the other side. The probability of transmission depends on the barrier height and width. In alpha decay: the alpha particle exists as a cluster within the nucleus, trapped by the strong nuclear force (attractive well) surrounded by a Coulomb repulsion barrier (the electrostatic repulsion between the alpha and remaining nucleus). Classically, the alpha cannot escape. Quantum mechanically, the wavefunction tunnels through the Coulomb barrier with a small but non-zero probability. Over time (half-life depends on tunnelling probability), the alpha particle escapes. Thin, low barriers give higher tunnelling probability and shorter half-lives; thick, high barriers give longer half-lives.

quiz.question: Describe the double-slit experiment performed with single electrons and explain what it implies about the nature of quantum objects.
quiz.markscheme: In the electron double-slit experiment, a beam of electrons (or even individual electrons fired one at a time) passes through two narrow slits and lands on a detector screen. Individual electrons create single, localized flashes on the screen (particle-like). But after many electrons have been recorded, an interference pattern of alternating bands builds up — identical to that seen with light waves. Crucially, this pattern appears even when electrons are sent one at a time (ruling out electron–electron interaction). This means each electron must pass through both slits simultaneously as a wave (superposition of two paths) and interfere with itself. The probability of landing at any point is given by |ψ|², where ψ is the wavefunction. If a 'which-path' detector is placed at the slits, the interference pattern disappears — the act of measurement collapses the wavefunction. This illustrates wave–particle duality and the probabilistic, non-deterministic nature of quantum mechanics.

title: Radioactive decay
objectives: Describe the properties of alpha (α), beta-minus (β⁻), beta-plus (β⁺), and gamma (γ) radiations, including penetrating power and ionising ability.
Write balanced nuclear equations for alpha, beta-minus, beta-plus, and gamma decay.
Define the decay constant λ and apply the exponential decay law: N = N₀ e^(−λt) and A = A₀ e^(−λt).
Define half-life T½ and derive the relationship T½ = ln2/λ.
Explain radioactive decay series and describe safety precautions for handling radioactive sources.
Apply radioactive decay to carbon dating and other radiometric dating techniques.
summary:
Radioactive decay is the spontaneous and random disintegration of an unstable nucleus, emitting radiation. It cannot be influenced by temperature, pressure, or chemical state. Three principal types of radiation: alpha (α): two protons and two neutrons (helium-4 nucleus), charge +2e, stopped by a few centimetres of air or a sheet of paper, highly ionising. Beta-minus (β⁻): an electron emitted when a neutron converts to a proton (n → p + e⁻ + v̄_e), charge −e, stopped by a few mm of aluminium, moderately ionising. Beta-plus (β⁺): a positron emitted when a proton converts to a neutron (p → n + e⁺ + v_e), charge +e. Gamma (γ): high-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted after alpha or beta decay (nucleus de-excites), no charge or mass, stopped by many cm of lead or concrete, weakly ionising but deeply penetrating.

Nuclear equations conserve mass number (top) and charge number (bottom): alpha decay: ᴬ_ZX → ᴬ⁻⁴_(Z−2)Y + ⁴₂He; beta-minus: ᴬ_ZX → ᴬ_(Z+1)Y + e⁻ + v̄_e; beta-plus: ᴬ_ZX → ᴬ_(Z−1)Y + e⁺ + v_e; gamma emission does not change A or Z.

The exponential decay law arises because each nucleus has the same probability λ (decay constant, s⁻¹) of decaying per unit time, independent of age: dN/dt = −λN, giving N = N₀ e^(−λt). Activity A = λN = A₀ e^(−λt). Half-life T½ is the time for half the nuclei to decay: T½ = ln2/λ = 0.693/λ. After n half-lives, N = N₀(½)ⁿ. Activity A (becquerel, Bq = decays per second).

Radioactive decay series: heavy nuclei (e.g. U-238) undergo a sequence of alpha and beta decays, terminating at a stable nucleus (e.g. Pb-206). Carbon-14 dating uses the known T½ of ¹⁴C (5730 years) and the constant ratio of ¹⁴C/¹²C in living organisms; once an organism dies, ¹⁴C decays and the ratio decreases — measuring the ratio gives the age. Other radiometric methods use potassium-40 (T½ = 1.3 Gy) for geological dating.

quiz.question: Compare alpha, beta, and gamma radiation in terms of charge, mass, ionising ability, and penetrating power.
quiz.markscheme: Alpha (α): charge +2e, mass number 4 (helium nucleus), highest ionising ability (creates about 10 000 ion pairs per mm in air) but lowest penetrating power (stopped by ~5 cm of air or a sheet of paper). Beta (β⁻): charge −1e, mass ~1/2000 of proton, moderate ionisation, stopped by ~3 mm of aluminium. Gamma (γ): no charge, no mass, weakest ionisation but highest penetrating power (attenuated, never fully stopped, by many cm of lead or concrete). Alpha is most dangerous if source is inside the body; gamma is most dangerous from external sources.

quiz.question: Write balanced nuclear equations for (a) alpha decay of uranium-238 and (b) beta-minus decay of carbon-14.
quiz.markscheme: (a) Alpha decay of ²³⁸₉₂U: ²³⁸₉₂U → ²³⁴₉₀Th + ⁴₂He. Check: A: 238 = 234 + 4 ✓; Z: 92 = 90 + 2 ✓. Thorium-234 is the daughter. (b) Beta-minus decay of ¹⁴₆C: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e + v̄_e. Check: A: 14 = 14 + 0 ✓; Z: 6 = 7 − 1 ✓. Nitrogen-14 is the daughter. (The antineutrino v̄_e is also emitted but does not affect A or Z.)

quiz.question: A radioactive source has an initial activity of 4000 Bq and a half-life of 8.0 days. Calculate the activity after 24 days and the decay constant.
Derive the relationship between half-life T½ and decay constant λ.
quiz.markscheme: The decay law is N = N₀ e^(−λt). The half-life T½ is defined as the time when N = N₀/2. Substituting: N₀/2 = N₀ e^(−λT½). Dividing both sides by N₀: ½ = e^(−λT½). Taking natural log: ln(½) = −λT½ → −ln2 = −λT½ → T½ = ln2/λ = 0.693/λ. So λ = 0.693/T½. A larger decay constant means shorter half-life (faster decay); a smaller decay constant means longer half-life (slower decay).

quiz.question: A wooden artefact has a ¹⁴C activity of 15% of that expected from a living tree. Estimate its age. (T½ of ¹⁴C = 5730 years)
quiz.markscheme: Using A = A₀ e^(−λt) → 0.15 A₀ = A₀ e^(−λt) → 0.15 = e^(−λt). Taking ln: ln(0.15) = −λt → −1.897 = −λt. λ = 0.693/5730 = 1.21 × 10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹. t = 1.897/(1.21 × 10⁻⁴) = 15 677 yr ≈ 15 700 years. The artefact is approximately 15 700 years old. Note: carbon dating assumes the atmospheric ¹⁴C/¹²C ratio has been approximately constant, which requires calibration against tree rings (dendrochronology) for precision.

quiz.question: Explain why radioactive decay is described as 'random and spontaneous' and describe one safety precaution appropriate for handling a gamma source.
quiz.markscheme: Random: it is impossible to predict which particular nucleus in a sample will decay next, or when. Each nucleus has the same probability of decaying per unit time (described by λ), regardless of its history — decay follows a Poisson distribution; the actual count rate fluctuates around the mean. Spontaneous: decay occurs without any external trigger; it is unaffected by temperature, pressure, chemical environment, or electromagnetic fields. Safety for gamma source: maximise distance from the source (intensity ∝ 1/d² — inverse square law; doubling distance reduces intensity by factor 4); minimize exposure time; use lead shielding (many cm of lead significantly attenuates gamma); use remote handling tools (tongs, not hands); monitor dose with a Geiger counter or dosimeter.
